# Coordination: Stigmergy, Pheromones, and Collective Intelligence

> **Topic**: 13-coordination
>
> **Scope**: Multi-agent coordination in Roko — stigmergy theory, digital pheromones,
> pheromone types and scoping, Agent Mesh transport, morphogenetic specialization,
> exponential flywheel mechanisms, collective intelligence metrics, and current
> implementation status.

---

## Summary

This topic covers how Roko agents coordinate without direct communication, using **stigmergy**
— indirect coordination through environmental modification. The environment in Roko is a
multi-layered pheromone field composed of typed, decaying, scoped Engrams that agents deposit
and sense. These digital pheromones carry coordination signals (threats, opportunities,
patterns, wisdom) that propagate through three scopes (Local, Mesh, Global) via the Agent Mesh
transport layer (WebSocket relay, Iroh P2P, ERC-8004 discovery).

On top of the pheromone field, **morphogenetic specialization** uses Turing reaction-diffusion
dynamics to produce emergent role differentiation in Collectives of initially identical agents.
Activation (profitable strategies reinforce slowly via experience) and inhibition (Collective-
wide pheromone signals propagate fast via the Agent Mesh) satisfy Turing's instability
condition, causing stable specialist patterns to emerge without central role assignment.

The **exponential flywheel** describes ten mechanisms (autocatalytic knowledge networks,
superlinear scaling, Reed's Law, knowledge distillation cascades, evolutionary dynamics,
collective calibration, cross-domain resonance, niche construction, information-theoretic
compounding, transactive memory) that produce superlinear growth in collective intelligence.
The **C-Factor** and composite **C-Score** metrics quantify coordination effectiveness.

---

## Prerequisites

- Familiarity with Roko's Synapse Architecture (6 traits: Substrate, Scorer, Gate, Router,
  Composer, Policy) — see `00-architecture/`
- Understanding of the Engram type (content-addressed, scored, decaying unit of cognition) —
  see `00-architecture/`
- Basic awareness of the 5-layer taxonomy (L0 Runtime through L4 Orchestration) — see
  `00-architecture/`

---

| # | File | Title | Lines | Summary |
|---|------|-------|-------|---------|
| 00 | [00-stigmergy-theory.md](00-stigmergy-theory.md) | Stigmergy Theory | ~260 | Grassé 1959, formal definition, two forms (sematectonic/marker-based), why stigmergy over direct communication, scalability/robustness/asynchrony analysis, Grossman-Stiglitz paradox resolution |
| 01 | [01-stigmergy-beyond-termites.md](01-stigmergy-beyond-termites.md) | Stigmergy Beyond Termites | ~280 | Biological examples (ants, bees, bacteria, spiders), human stigmergy (Wikipedia, science, cities, OSS), computational stigmergy (ACO, PSO, swarm robotics), software engineering (code smells, niche construction, information foraging), Constructal Law, self-organized criticality |
| 02 | [02-git-as-stigmergy.md](02-git-as-stigmergy.md) | Git as Stigmergy | ~250 | Repository as stigmergic environment, sematectonic (code structure) and marker-based (commits, branches, CI) signals, multi-agent worktree model, base+overlay pattern, declared contracts, pheromone traces in codebases |
| 03 | [03-digital-pheromones.md](03-digital-pheromones.md) | Digital Pheromones | ~280 | Pheromone struct, exponential decay formula, decay profiles per kind, confirmation mechanics, anti-spoofing via reputation, pheromone field operations, pheromone-enriched context assembly, complete lifecycle |
| 04 | [04-pheromone-kinds.md](04-pheromone-kinds.md) | Pheromone Kinds | ~280 | PheromoneKind enum (Threat/Opportunity/Wisdom/Alpha/Pattern/Anomaly/Consensus/Custom), three-tier taxonomy, kind interactions, Alpha paradox, Pattern→Wisdom promotion, Consensus stability |
| 05 | [05-pheromone-scope.md](05-pheromone-scope.md) | Pheromone Scope | ~260 | PheromoneScope enum (Local/Mesh/Global), three-level hierarchy, Constructal Law connection, trust discounting, scope promotion, cross-scope composition, permissioned subnets preview |
| 06 | [06-agent-mesh-sync.md](06-agent-mesh-sync.md) | Agent Mesh Sync | ~270 | Dual-transport architecture (WebSocket + Iroh), EventFabric integration, connection registry, store-and-forward, iroh-gossip pheromone propagation, iroh-blobs knowledge exchange, ERC-8004 discovery, failure modes, security model |
| 07 | [07-morphogenetic-specialization.md](07-morphogenetic-specialization.md) | Morphogenetic Specialization | ~300 | Turing 1952 reaction-diffusion, strategy concentration vector (8 dimensions), Gierer-Meinhardt kinetics, update rule with activation/inhibition/decay/noise, niche competition, role coordination messages, resource pressure modulation, convergence analysis, DeLanda assemblage theory |
| 08 | [08-permissioned-subnets.md](08-permissioned-subnets.md) | Permissioned Subnets | ~240 | Private Mesh scopes, access control models (invite/role/reputation), internal reputation, opt-in publishing, scope boundary enforcement, organizational patterns, club goods theory |
| 09 | [09-stigmergy-scaling.md](09-stigmergy-scaling.md) | Stigmergy Scaling | ~250 | O(N×M) vs O(N²) analysis, pheromone field scaling, transport scaling (relay vs gossip), morphogenetic scaling, knowledge sync scaling, practical limits, comparison with consensus and pub-sub |
| 10 | [10-exponential-flywheel.md](10-exponential-flywheel.md) | Exponential Flywheel | ~280 | 10 mechanisms for superlinear growth: autocatalytic networks (Kauffman), superlinear scaling (West/Bettencourt β≈1.15), Reed's Law (2^N), distillation cascades (Hinton), evolutionary dynamics, collective calibration (31.6×), cross-domain resonance (HDC 0.526), niche construction, information-theoretic compounding, transactive memory |
| 11 | [11-collective-intelligence-metrics.md](11-collective-intelligence-metrics.md) | Collective Intelligence Metrics | ~280 | C-Factor (Woolley et al. 2010), composite C-Score (4 diagnostic signals), turn-taking equality, knowledge flow rate, cross-domain transfer, emergent coordination, information-theoretic metrics, A/B testing with clustered standard errors, dashboard integration |

---

1→# Stigmergy Theory: Indirect Coordination Through Environmental Modification
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L4 Orchestration (coordination mechanisms), with cross-cuts into L0 Runtime (event
     4→> persistence) and L1 Framework (transport)
     5→>
     6→> **Synapse traits**: `Substrate` (store pheromone Engrams), `Scorer` (rate pheromone intensity),
     7→> `Policy` (observe pheromone streams, emit reactive Engrams)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: `13-coordination/INDEX.md` (overview), familiarity with the Engram struct
    10→> (Roko's content-addressed, scored, decaying unit of cognition)
    11→
    12→
    13→> **Implementation**: Specified
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## What Is Stigmergy?
    18→
    19→Stigmergy is a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents, where the trace left in the
    20→environment by one agent's action stimulates the performance of a subsequent action by the same
    21→or a different agent. The term was coined by French entomologist Pierre-Paul Grassé in 1959 to
    22→describe how termites coordinate the construction of elaborate mound structures without any
    23→centralized plan, blueprint, or direct communication between individuals [Grassé, P.-P. "La
    24→Reconstruction du Nid et les Coordinations Inter-Individuelles chez Bellicositermes Natalensis
    25→et Cubitermes sp." *Insectes Sociaux*, 6(1):41-80, 1959].
    26→
    27→The core insight is deceptively simple: **agents do not need to communicate with each other
    28→directly. They only need to read from and write to a shared environment.** The environment
    29→itself becomes the coordination medium.
    30→
    31→### The Termite Example
    32→
    33→Grassé observed that termites building a mound follow no central plan. Instead:
    34→
    35→1. A termite picks up a mud pellet and deposits it at a location.
    36→2. The deposited pellet contains a chemical pheromone that attracts other termites.
    37→3. Attracted termites deposit their own pellets nearby, reinforcing the pheromone signal.
    38→4. The growing pile of pellets, through its physical shape and pheromone concentration,
    39→   guides further construction — arches form, chambers emerge, ventilation shafts develop.
    40→5. No termite has a model of the whole structure. Each termite responds to local stimuli.
    41→
    42→The structure that emerges is far more complex than any individual termite could plan. This is
    43→the hallmark of stigmergy: **simple local rules + persistent environmental modification =
    44→complex global behavior**.
    45→
    46→### Formal Definition
    47→
    48→Stigmergy requires three conditions [Theraulaz, G. & Bonabeau, E. "A Brief History of
    49→Stigmergy." *Artificial Life*, 5(2):97-116, 1999]:
    50→
    51→| Condition | Description | Roko Equivalent |
    52→|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
    53→| **Shared environment** | All agents can read from and write to a common medium | NeuroStore (local Substrate), Agent Mesh (peer network), Korai chain (global ledger) |
    54→| **Persistent modifications** | Agent actions leave traces that outlast the agent's presence | Engrams with configurable decay rates (2h–∞) |
    55→| **Stimulus-response coupling** | Traces in the environment trigger specific behaviors in agents that encounter them | `Policy` trait implementations that react to scored Engrams |
    56→
    57→When all three conditions are met, coordination emerges without any agent needing a global view,
    58→without any central coordinator, and without agents needing to know about each other's existence.
    59→
    60→---
    61→
    62→## Two Forms of Stigmergy
    63→
    64→Grassé and subsequent researchers identified two distinct forms of stigmergy, both of which
    65→appear in Roko's architecture [Holland, O. & Melhuish, C. "Stigmergy, Self-Organization, and
    66→Sorting in Collective Robotics." *Artificial Life*, 5(2):173-202, 1999]:
    67→
    68→### Sematectonic Stigmergy (Structure-Based)
    69→
    70→In sematectonic stigmergy, the **physical structure** created by agents guides subsequent work.
    71→The product itself is the signal. Grassé observed this in termite mound construction: the shape
    72→of a partially built arch tells the next termite where to place its mud pellet. No chemical
    73→signal is needed — the geometry of the structure is the coordination mechanism.
    74→
    75→**In Roko**: The codebase itself is sematectonic stigmergy. When a coding agent writes a
    76→function, the function's signature, its location in the module hierarchy, its documentation, and
    77→its test coverage all constitute structural signals that guide subsequent agents. A well-typed
    78→function with clear documentation "invites" usage; a function with poor error handling "invites"
    79→improvement. The code structure guides agent behavior without any explicit communication.
    80→
    81→Examples of sematectonic stigmergy in software development:
    82→
    83→| Structural Feature | Signal Conveyed | Agent Response |
    84→|-------------------|----------------|----------------|
    85→| Empty test file | "Tests needed here" | Testing agent writes tests |
    86→| `TODO` comment | "Incomplete implementation" | Coding agent completes it |
    87→| Unused import | "Stale code" | Refactoring agent cleans up |
    88→| Well-documented API | "Ready for integration" | Integration agent uses it |
    89→| Failing CI badge | "Broken build" | Debugging agent investigates |
    90→| Missing error handling | "Fragile code path" | Hardening agent adds handling |
    91→
    92→### Marker-Based Stigmergy (Signal-Based)
    93→
    94→In marker-based stigmergy, agents deposit **explicit signals** (markers or pheromones) in the
    95→environment. These signals carry information beyond the physical structure — they encode
    96→urgency, type, confidence, and decay over time. Ant trail pheromones are the canonical example:
    97→a foraging ant that finds food deposits a trail pheromone on its return path, and the
    98→pheromone's concentration encodes the quality and proximity of the food source.
    99→
   100→**In Roko**: Digital pheromones — typed Engrams with explicit `kind`, `intensity`, `scope`, and
   101→exponential decay profiles — implement marker-based stigmergy. When an agent detects a threat
   102→(e.g., a failing test suite, an anomalous metric, a security vulnerability), it deposits a
   103→`Threat` pheromone that other agents can sense and react to. The pheromone decays over time
   104→(threat signals have a 2-hour half-life), so stale threats don't permanently distort behavior.
   105→
   106→Key properties of marker-based stigmergy in Roko:
   107→
   108→1. **Typed signals**: Different pheromone kinds trigger different agent responses (see
   109→   `04-pheromone-kinds.md`).
   110→2. **Exponential decay**: Signals lose intensity over time, preventing stale information from
   111→   accumulating (see `03-digital-pheromones.md`).
   112→3. **Confirmation reinforcement**: When multiple agents deposit the same pheromone type at the
   113→   same location, the effective half-life extends, making well-confirmed signals persist longer.
   114→4. **Scope control**: Pheromones can be local (one Substrate), mesh-wide (one Collective), or
   115→   global (public Korai chain) — see `05-pheromone-scope.md`.
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## Why Stigmergy, Not Direct Communication?
   120→
   121→Direct agent-to-agent communication (message passing, shared blackboards, leader election) has
   122→been the dominant paradigm in multi-agent systems since the 1980s [Hewitt, C. "Viewing Control
   123→Structures as Patterns of Passing Messages." *Artificial Intelligence*, 8(3):323-364, 1977].
   124→Roko uses stigmergy instead for several fundamental reasons, each grounded in empirical research.
   125→
   126→### Scalability
   127→
   128→Direct communication scales as O(N²) for N agents — every agent must potentially communicate
   129→with every other agent. Stigmergy scales as O(N × M) where M is the number of distinct signal
   130→types (pheromone kinds), which is bounded and small. In Roko, M = 7 universal kinds + domain
   131→extensions, so coordination cost grows linearly with the number of agents.
   132→
   133→| Agents (N) | Direct Comm (O(N²)) | Stigmergy (O(N × M), M=10) |
   134→|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
   135→| 5 | 25 channels | 50 read/writes |
   136→| 50 | 2,500 channels | 500 read/writes |
   137→| 500 | 250,000 channels | 5,000 read/writes |
   138→| 5,000 | 25,000,000 channels | 50,000 read/writes |
   139→
   140→> "Stigmergy provides a clear separation between the coordination mechanism and the individual
   141→> agents, allowing the system to scale without modifying agent behavior." — [Parunak, H.V.D.
   142→> "Go to the Ant: Engineering Principles from Natural Multi-Agent Systems." *Annals of
   143→> Operations Research*, 75:69-101, 1997]
   144→
   145→### Robustness
   146→
   147→In a direct communication system, the failure of a key node (coordinator, leader, message
   148→broker) can paralyze the entire collective. Stigmergy is inherently robust because the
   149→coordination state is distributed across the environment, not concentrated in any single agent.
   150→If an agent fails:
   151→
   152→- Its previously deposited pheromones persist and continue to guide other agents.
   153→- No other agent needs to be notified of the failure.
   154→- The pheromone field naturally adapts as the failed agent's signals decay.
   155→- New agents joining the collective can immediately sense the current state.
   156→
   157→This property is formalized in the concept of **graceful degradation**: the collective's
   158→performance degrades smoothly with agent loss, rather than failing catastrophically
   159→[Bonabeau, E., Dorigo, M. & Theraulaz, G. *Swarm Intelligence: From Natural to Artificial
   160→Systems*. Oxford University Press, 1999].
   161→
   162→### Asynchrony
   163→
   164→Stigmergy is inherently asynchronous. The depositing agent and the sensing agent do not need
   165→to be active at the same time. A pheromone deposited at time T can influence an agent at time
   166→T + Δt, where Δt can be seconds, minutes, or hours. This is essential for Roko's multi-speed
   167→cognitive architecture:
   168→
   169→| Cognitive Speed | Tick Duration | Stigmergy Role |
   170→|----------------|---------------|----------------|
   171→| T0 (System-1, fast) | ~15 seconds | Sense ambient pheromones, react to high-intensity signals |
   172→| T1 (System-2, deliberate) | ~60 seconds | Analyze pheromone patterns, deposit new observations |
   173→| T2 (Reflective) | ~5 minutes | Consolidate pheromone history, emit Wisdom Engrams |
   174→
   175→An agent running at T0 speed can sense pheromones deposited by a T2 agent hours earlier. The
   176→decoupling of production and consumption in time is a fundamental advantage over synchronous
   177→communication protocols.
   178→
   179→### Minimal Agent Complexity
   180→
   181→Each agent only needs to implement two operations:
   182→
   183→1. **Deposit**: Write an Engram to the Substrate with a pheromone kind and intensity.
   184→2. **Sense**: Query the Substrate for nearby Engrams above a threshold intensity.
   185→
   186→The agent does not need to know how many other agents exist, what strategies they follow, or
   187→whether they are online. This dramatically reduces the complexity of individual agents while
   188→enabling sophisticated collective behavior.
   189→
   190→In terms of the Synapse Architecture, deposit maps to the `Substrate::store()` trait method,
   191→and sense maps to `Substrate::query()` followed by `Scorer::score()` to rank the sensed
   192→pheromones by relevance. The `Policy` trait then observes the scored pheromone stream and
   193→decides whether to emit a reactive Engram (closing the stigmergic loop).
   194→
   195→---
   196→
   197→## Stigmergy in Computer Science
   198→
   199→While Grassé studied biological systems, the principles of stigmergy have been applied
   200→extensively in computer science, particularly in swarm intelligence and distributed optimization.
   201→
   202→### Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)
   203→
   204→Dorigo's Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) is the most prominent computational application of
   205→stigmergy [Dorigo, M., Maniezzo, V. & Colorni, A. "Ant System: Optimization by a Colony of
   206→Cooperating Agents." *IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics B*, 26(1):29-41,
   207→1996]. In ACO:
   208→
   209→1. Artificial ants traverse a graph (e.g., the Travelling Salesman Problem).
   210→2. Each ant deposits pheromone on the edges it traverses, proportional to the quality of
   211→   its solution.
   212→3. Subsequent ants probabilistically prefer edges with higher pheromone concentration.
   213→4. Pheromone decays over time (evaporation), preventing premature convergence.
   214→5. The collective converges on high-quality solutions without any ant having a global view.
   215→
   216→ACO has been applied to routing, scheduling, assignment problems, and protein folding. Its
   217→success demonstrates that stigmergy is not just a biological curiosity — it is a **general
   218→coordination principle** applicable to any domain where agents must collectively optimize in a
   219→large search space.
   220→
   221→### Digital Pheromone Systems
   222→
   223→Parunak extended stigmergy to general software agent systems with the concept of digital
   224→pheromones — software analogs of chemical pheromones that are deposited, sensed, and decay in
   225→a shared computational environment [Parunak, H.V.D., Brueckner, S.A. & Sauter, J.A. "Digital
   226→Pheromones for Coordination of Unmanned Vehicles." *Environments for Multi-Agent Systems*,
   227→LNCS 3374:246-263, Springer, 2005].
   228→
   229→Key properties identified by Parunak:
   230→
   231→| Property | Biological | Digital (Roko) |
   232→|----------|-----------|----------------|
   233→| Deposition | Chemical secretion | `Substrate::store(Engram { kind: PheromoneKind::Threat, ... })` |
   234→| Diffusion | Brownian motion through medium | Mesh gossip propagation (see `06-agent-mesh-sync.md`) |
   235→| Evaporation | Chemical degradation | Exponential decay: `intensity(t) = base × e^(-0.693 × elapsed / τ)` |
   236→| Sensing | Chemoreceptors | `Substrate::query()` with pheromone kind filter |
   237→| Reinforcement | Multiple depositions | Confirmation count extends effective half-life |
   238→
   239→### Swarm Intelligence in Distributed Systems
   240→
   241→Beyond ACO, stigmergy has been applied to:
   242→
   243→- **Load balancing**: Agents leave "workload" pheromones; overloaded nodes repel new tasks,
   244→  underloaded nodes attract them [Gupta, D. et al. "Online Load Balancing via Swarm
   245→  Intelligence." *Autonomous Agents and Multi-Agent Systems*, 8(2):209-229, 2004].
   246→- **Network routing**: Ant-based routing protocols (AntNet) achieve adaptive routing in
   247→  telecommunications networks [Di Caro, G. & Dorigo, M. "AntNet: Distributed Stigmergic
   248→  Control for Communications Networks." *JAIR*, 9:317-365, 1998].
   249→- **Collaborative filtering**: User actions (purchases, ratings) serve as implicit pheromones
   250→  that guide other users' discovery [Leskovec, J. et al. "The Dynamics of Viral Marketing."
   251→  *ACM TWEB*, 1(1):5, 2007].
   252→- **Version control**: Git repositories are stigmergic environments — commits, branches, and
   253→  merge patterns guide future development (see `02-git-as-stigmergy.md`).
   254→
   255→---
   256→
   257→## Stigmergy in Roko's Architecture
   258→
   259→Roko implements stigmergy at multiple architectural layers, making it a first-class
   260→coordination primitive rather than an afterthought.
   261→
   262→### Layer Mapping
   263→
   264→| Layer | Stigmergic Component | Implementation |
   265→|-------|---------------------|----------------|
   266→| L0 Runtime | Engram persistence, decay timers, event emission | `roko-fs` (FileSubstrate JSONL), adaptive clock |
   267→| L1 Framework | Pheromone type system, transport backends | `PheromoneKind` enum, `PheromoneScope` enum, WebSocket/Iroh/ERC-8004 |
   268→| L2 Scaffold | Pheromone-enriched context assembly | Context composer includes ambient pheromone summary |
   269→| L3 Harness | Pheromone-based gate thresholds | Threat pheromone concentration adjusts gate strictness |
   270→| L4 Orchestration | Multi-agent pheromone coordination, morphogenetic specialization | Collective-level pheromone field, reaction-diffusion dynamics |
   271→
   272→### The Stigmergic Loop
   273→
   274→The complete stigmergic loop in Roko follows this sequence:
   275→
   276→```
   277→Agent A acts → deposits pheromone Engram to Substrate
   278→    ↓
   279→Engram propagates (local → Mesh → Global based on scope)
   280→    ↓
   281→Agent B queries Substrate → senses pheromone
   282→    ↓
   283→Scorer rates pheromone intensity and relevance
   284→    ↓
   285→Router selects highest-priority pheromone signal
   286→    ↓
   287→Agent B acts in response → deposits its own pheromone Engram
   288→    ↓
   289→(cycle continues — emergent coordination without direct communication)
   290→```
   291→
   292→This loop is isomorphic to Roko's universal cognitive loop (query → score → route → compose →
   293→act → verify → write → react), with pheromone Engrams serving as the coordination substrate.
   294→
   295→### Three Knowledge Scopes as Stigmergic Layers
   296→
   297→Roko's three-level knowledge architecture maps directly to three stigmergic scopes (see
   298→`05-pheromone-scope.md` for full details):
   299→
   300→| Scope | Environment | Persistence | Audience | Example |
   301→|-------|-------------|-------------|----------|---------|
   302→| `Local(SubstrateId)` | Agent's own NeuroStore | Infinite (until GC) | Self only | "I found a bug in module X" |
   303→| `Mesh(CollectiveId)` | Collective's Agent Mesh | Configurable (hours–days) | Collective members | "Module X has a regression" |
   304→| `Global` | Korai chain (public) | Permanent (on-chain) | All agents | "CVE-2026-XXXX affects dependency Y" |
   305→
   306→Each scope represents a different stigmergic environment with different persistence
   307→characteristics, audience sizes, and trust levels. Information flows upward through promotion
   308→gates: a local observation can be promoted to Mesh scope after confidence validation, and from
   309→Mesh to Global after collective confirmation.
   310→
   311→---
   312→
   313→## The Grossman-Stiglitz Paradox and Pheromone Economics
   314→
   315→A fundamental challenge in any information-sharing system is the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox:
   316→if information is freely available, no agent has an incentive to incur the cost of producing
   317→it [Grossman, S.J. & Stiglitz, J.E. "On the Impossibility of Informationally Efficient
   318→Markets." *American Economic Review*, 70(3):393-408, 1980].
   319→
   320→Roko resolves this paradox through the pheromone system's natural properties:
   321→
   322→1. **Decay creates scarcity**: Pheromones decay exponentially. Information that was freely
   323→   sensed yesterday may no longer be available today. Agents that produce fresh signals provide
   324→   genuine value to the collective.
   325→
   326→2. **Confirmation extends value**: An agent that confirms another agent's pheromone (by
   327→   depositing the same kind at the same scope) extends the effective half-life. This is a
   328→   cooperative act that benefits the confirming agent (stronger signal for its own decisions)
   329→   and the collective (more persistent shared knowledge).
   330→
   331→3. **Reputation tracks contribution**: Agents that consistently produce high-quality pheromones
   332→   (signals that are later confirmed rather than contradicted) accumulate reputation, which
   333→   affects their routing priority and resource allocation.
   334→
   335→4. **Domain scoping prevents free-riding**: Pheromones are scoped to specific domains. An
   336→   agent must be active in a domain to sense its pheromones, creating natural communities of
   337→   practice where contributors and consumers overlap.
   338→
   339→---
   340→
   341→## Research Foundations
   342→
   343→The theoretical foundations of stigmergy draw from multiple disciplines:
   344→
   345→### Entomology and Ethology
   346→
   347→- [Grassé 1959] — Original observation of stigmergy in termite mound construction
   348→- [Wilson, E.O. "The Insect Societies." Belknap Press, 1971] — Comprehensive treatment of
   349→  social insect coordination mechanisms
   350→- [Hölldobler, B. & Wilson, E.O. "The Superorganism." W.W. Norton, 2008] — Superorganism
   351→  theory and multi-level coordination in ant colonies
   352→- [Camazine, S. et al. "Self-Organization in Biological Systems." Princeton University Press,
   353→  2001] — General principles of biological self-organization
   354→
   355→### Swarm Intelligence
   356→
   357→- [Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz 1999] — *Swarm Intelligence: From Natural to Artificial
   358→  Systems*, the definitive bridge between biological stigmergy and computational applications
   359→- [Dorigo, Maniezzo & Colorni 1996] — Ant Colony Optimization, the first major computational
   360→  application of stigmergy
   361→- [Kennedy, J. & Eberhart, R. "Particle Swarm Optimization." *IEEE ICNN*, 1995] — Related
   362→  swarm intelligence paradigm
   363→
   364→### Multi-Agent Systems
   365→
   366→- [Parunak 1997] — "Go to the Ant" — engineering principles from biological multi-agent systems
   367→- [Parunak, Brueckner & Sauter 2005] — Digital pheromones for coordination
   368→- [Theraulaz & Bonabeau 1999] — History and formalization of stigmergy
   369→
   370→### Distributed Systems
   371→
   372→- [Lamport, L. "Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System." *CACM*,
   373→  21(7), 1978] — Foundation for version vectors used in pheromone deduplication across transports
   374→- [Fidge, C.J. "Timestamps in Message-Passing Systems." *ACSC*, 10(1), 1988] — Vector clock
   375→  formalization applied to Engram sequence tracking
   376→
   377→---
   378→
   379→## Summary
   380→
   381→Stigmergy is Roko's primary coordination mechanism because it provides:
   382→
   383→1. **Linear scalability** — O(N × M) vs O(N²) for direct communication
   384→2. **Inherent robustness** — no single point of failure, graceful degradation
   385→3. **Temporal decoupling** — producers and consumers operate asynchronously
   386→4. **Minimal agent complexity** — deposit + sense, no knowledge of other agents needed
   387→5. **Natural information economics** — decay, confirmation, and reputation resolve the
   388→   Grossman-Stiglitz paradox
   389→
   390→The next sub-docs detail how these principles are instantiated in Roko's digital pheromone
   391→system (`03-digital-pheromones.md`), the specific pheromone types (`04-pheromone-kinds.md`),
   392→scope model (`05-pheromone-scope.md`), and transport layer (`06-agent-mesh-sync.md`).
   393→
   394→---
   395→
   396→## Related Sub-Docs
   397→
   398→- `01-stigmergy-beyond-termites.md` — Stigmergy in non-biological systems
   399→- `02-git-as-stigmergy.md` — Version control as a stigmergic environment
   400→- `03-digital-pheromones.md` — Roko's typed pheromone Engram system
   401→- `07-morphogenetic-specialization.md` — Turing reaction-diffusion for role emergence

# Stigmergy Theory: Indirect Coordination Through Environmental Modification

> **Layer**: L4 Orchestration (coordination mechanisms), with cross-cuts into L0 Runtime (event
> persistence) and L1 Framework (transport)
>
> **Synapse traits**: `Substrate` (store pheromone Engrams), `Scorer` (rate pheromone intensity),
> `Policy` (observe pheromone streams, emit reactive Engrams)
>
> **Prerequisites**: `13-coordination/INDEX.md` (overview), familiarity with the Engram struct
> (Roko's content-addressed, scored, decaying unit of cognition)


> **Implementation**: Specified

---

## What Is Stigmergy?

Stigmergy is a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents, where the trace left in the
environment by one agent's action stimulates the performance of a subsequent action by the same
or a different agent. The term was coined by French entomologist Pierre-Paul Grassé in 1959 to
describe how termites coordinate the construction of elaborate mound structures without any
centralized plan, blueprint, or direct communication between individuals [Grassé, P.-P. "La
Reconstruction du Nid et les Coordinations Inter-Individuelles chez Bellicositermes Natalensis
et Cubitermes sp." *Insectes Sociaux*, 6(1):41-80, 1959].

The core insight is deceptively simple: **agents do not need to communicate with each other
directly. They only need to read from and write to a shared environment.** The environment
itself becomes the coordination medium.

### The Termite Example

Grassé observed that termites building a mound follow no central plan. Instead:

1. A termite picks up a mud pellet and deposits it at a location.
2. The deposited pellet contains a chemical pheromone that attracts other termites.
3. Attracted termites deposit their own pellets nearby, reinforcing the pheromone signal.
4. The growing pile of pellets, through its physical shape and pheromone concentration,
   guides further construction — arches form, chambers emerge, ventilation shafts develop.
5. No termite has a model of the whole structure. Each termite responds to local stimuli.

The structure that emerges is far more complex than any individual termite could plan. This is
the hallmark of stigmergy: **simple local rules + persistent environmental modification =
complex global behavior**.

### Formal Definition

Stigmergy requires three conditions [Theraulaz, G. & Bonabeau, E. "A Brief History of
Stigmergy." *Artificial Life*, 5(2):97-116, 1999]:

| Condition | Description | Roko Equivalent |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Shared environment** | All agents can read from and write to a common medium | NeuroStore (local Substrate), Agent Mesh (peer network), Korai chain (global ledger) |
| **Persistent modifications** | Agent actions leave traces that outlast the agent's presence | Engrams with configurable decay rates (2h–∞) |
| **Stimulus-response coupling** | Traces in the environment trigger specific behaviors in agents that encounter them | `Policy` trait implementations that react to scored Engrams |

When all three conditions are met, coordination emerges without any agent needing a global view,
without any central coordinator, and without agents needing to know about each other's existence.

---

## Two Forms of Stigmergy

Grassé and subsequent researchers identified two distinct forms of stigmergy, both of which
appear in Roko's architecture [Holland, O. & Melhuish, C. "Stigmergy, Self-Organization, and
Sorting in Collective Robotics." *Artificial Life*, 5(2):173-202, 1999]:

### Sematectonic Stigmergy (Structure-Based)

In sematectonic stigmergy, the **physical structure** created by agents guides subsequent work.
The product itself is the signal. Grassé observed this in termite mound construction: the shape
of a partially built arch tells the next termite where to place its mud pellet. No chemical
signal is needed — the geometry of the structure is the coordination mechanism.

**In Roko**: The codebase itself is sematectonic stigmergy. When a coding agent writes a
function, the function's signature, its location in the module hierarchy, its documentation, and
its test coverage all constitute structural signals that guide subsequent agents. A well-typed
function with clear documentation "invites" usage; a function with poor error handling "invites"
improvement. The code structure guides agent behavior without any explicit communication.

Examples of sematectonic stigmergy in software development:

| Structural Feature | Signal Conveyed | Agent Response |
|-------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Empty test file | "Tests needed here" | Testing agent writes tests |
| `TODO` comment | "Incomplete implementation" | Coding agent completes it |
| Unused import | "Stale code" | Refactoring agent cleans up |
| Well-documented API | "Ready for integration" | Integration agent uses it |
| Failing CI badge | "Broken build" | Debugging agent investigates |
| Missing error handling | "Fragile code path" | Hardening agent adds handling |

### Marker-Based Stigmergy (Signal-Based)

In marker-based stigmergy, agents deposit **explicit signals** (markers or pheromones) in the
environment. These signals carry information beyond the physical structure — they encode
urgency, type, confidence, and decay over time. Ant trail pheromones are the canonical example:
a foraging ant that finds food deposits a trail pheromone on its return path, and the
pheromone's concentration encodes the quality and proximity of the food source.

**In Roko**: Digital pheromones — typed Engrams with explicit `kind`, `intensity`, `scope`, and
exponential decay profiles — implement marker-based stigmergy. When an agent detects a threat
(e.g., a failing test suite, an anomalous metric, a security vulnerability), it deposits a
`Threat` pheromone that other agents can sense and react to. The pheromone decays over time
(threat signals have a 2-hour half-life), so stale threats don't permanently distort behavior.

Key properties of marker-based stigmergy in Roko:

1. **Typed signals**: Different pheromone kinds trigger different agent responses (see
   `04-pheromone-kinds.md`).
2. **Exponential decay**: Signals lose intensity over time, preventing stale information from
   accumulating (see `03-digital-pheromones.md`).
3. **Confirmation reinforcement**: When multiple agents deposit the same pheromone type at the
   same location, the effective half-life extends, making well-confirmed signals persist longer.
4. **Scope control**: Pheromones can be local (one Substrate), mesh-wide (one Collective), or
   global (public Korai chain) — see `05-pheromone-scope.md`.

---

## Why Stigmergy, Not Direct Communication?

Direct agent-to-agent communication (message passing, shared blackboards, leader election) has
been the dominant paradigm in multi-agent systems since the 1980s [Hewitt, C. "Viewing Control
Structures as Patterns of Passing Messages." *Artificial Intelligence*, 8(3):323-364, 1977].
Roko uses stigmergy instead for several fundamental reasons, each grounded in empirical research.

### Scalability

Direct communication scales as O(N²) for N agents — every agent must potentially communicate
with every other agent. Stigmergy scales as O(N × M) where M is the number of distinct signal
types (pheromone kinds), which is bounded and small. In Roko, M = 7 universal kinds + domain
extensions, so coordination cost grows linearly with the number of agents.

| Agents (N) | Direct Comm (O(N²)) | Stigmergy (O(N × M), M=10) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 5 | 25 channels | 50 read/writes |
| 50 | 2,500 channels | 500 read/writes |
| 500 | 250,000 channels | 5,000 read/writes |
| 5,000 | 25,000,000 channels | 50,000 read/writes |

> "Stigmergy provides a clear separation between the coordination mechanism and the individual
> agents, allowing the system to scale without modifying agent behavior." — [Parunak, H.V.D.
> "Go to the Ant: Engineering Principles from Natural Multi-Agent Systems." *Annals of
> Operations Research*, 75:69-101, 1997]

### Robustness

In a direct communication system, the failure of a key node (coordinator, leader, message
broker) can paralyze the entire collective. Stigmergy is inherently robust because the
coordination state is distributed across the environment, not concentrated in any single agent.
If an agent fails:

- Its previously deposited pheromones persist and continue to guide other agents.
- No other agent needs to be notified of the failure.
- The pheromone field naturally adapts as the failed agent's signals decay.
- New agents joining the collective can immediately sense the current state.

This property is formalized in the concept of **graceful degradation**: the collective's
performance degrades smoothly with agent loss, rather than failing catastrophically
[Bonabeau, E., Dorigo, M. & Theraulaz, G. *Swarm Intelligence: From Natural to Artificial
Systems*. Oxford University Press, 1999].

### Asynchrony

Stigmergy is inherently asynchronous. The depositing agent and the sensing agent do not need
to be active at the same time. A pheromone deposited at time T can influence an agent at time
T + Δt, where Δt can be seconds, minutes, or hours. This is essential for Roko's multi-speed
cognitive architecture:

| Cognitive Speed | Tick Duration | Stigmergy Role |
|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| T0 (System-1, fast) | ~15 seconds | Sense ambient pheromones, react to high-intensity signals |
| T1 (System-2, deliberate) | ~60 seconds | Analyze pheromone patterns, deposit new observations |
| T2 (Reflective) | ~5 minutes | Consolidate pheromone history, emit Wisdom Engrams |

An agent running at T0 speed can sense pheromones deposited by a T2 agent hours earlier. The
decoupling of production and consumption in time is a fundamental advantage over synchronous
communication protocols.

### Minimal Agent Complexity

Each agent only needs to implement two operations:

1. **Deposit**: Write an Engram to the Substrate with a pheromone kind and intensity.
2. **Sense**: Query the Substrate for nearby Engrams above a threshold intensity.

The agent does not need to know how many other agents exist, what strategies they follow, or
whether they are online. This dramatically reduces the complexity of individual agents while
enabling sophisticated collective behavior.

In terms of the Synapse Architecture, deposit maps to the `Substrate::store()` trait method,
and sense maps to `Substrate::query()` followed by `Scorer::score()` to rank the sensed
pheromones by relevance. The `Policy` trait then observes the scored pheromone stream and
decides whether to emit a reactive Engram (closing the stigmergic loop).

---

## Stigmergy in Computer Science

While Grassé studied biological systems, the principles of stigmergy have been applied
extensively in computer science, particularly in swarm intelligence and distributed optimization.

### Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

Dorigo's Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) is the most prominent computational application of
stigmergy [Dorigo, M., Maniezzo, V. & Colorni, A. "Ant System: Optimization by a Colony of
Cooperating Agents." *IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics B*, 26(1):29-41,
1996]. In ACO:

1. Artificial ants traverse a graph (e.g., the Travelling Salesman Problem).
2. Each ant deposits pheromone on the edges it traverses, proportional to the quality of
   its solution.
3. Subsequent ants probabilistically prefer edges with higher pheromone concentration.
4. Pheromone decays over time (evaporation), preventing premature convergence.
5. The collective converges on high-quality solutions without any ant having a global view.

ACO has been applied to routing, scheduling, assignment problems, and protein folding. Its
success demonstrates that stigmergy is not just a biological curiosity — it is a **general
coordination principle** applicable to any domain where agents must collectively optimize in a
large search space.

### Digital Pheromone Systems

Parunak extended stigmergy to general software agent systems with the concept of digital
pheromones — software analogs of chemical pheromones that are deposited, sensed, and decay in
a shared computational environment [Parunak, H.V.D., Brueckner, S.A. & Sauter, J.A. "Digital
Pheromones for Coordination of Unmanned Vehicles." *Environments for Multi-Agent Systems*,
LNCS 3374:246-263, Springer, 2005].

Key properties identified by Parunak:

| Property | Biological | Digital (Roko) |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Deposition | Chemical secretion | `Substrate::store(Engram { kind: PheromoneKind::Threat, ... })` |
| Diffusion | Brownian motion through medium | Mesh gossip propagation (see `06-agent-mesh-sync.md`) |
| Evaporation | Chemical degradation | Exponential decay: `intensity(t) = base × e^(-0.693 × elapsed / τ)` |
| Sensing | Chemoreceptors | `Substrate::query()` with pheromone kind filter |
| Reinforcement | Multiple depositions | Confirmation count extends effective half-life |

### Swarm Intelligence in Distributed Systems

Beyond ACO, stigmergy has been applied to:

- **Load balancing**: Agents leave "workload" pheromones; overloaded nodes repel new tasks,
  underloaded nodes attract them [Gupta, D. et al. "Online Load Balancing via Swarm
  Intelligence." *Autonomous Agents and Multi-Agent Systems*, 8(2):209-229, 2004].
- **Network routing**: Ant-based routing protocols (AntNet) achieve adaptive routing in
  telecommunications networks [Di Caro, G. & Dorigo, M. "AntNet: Distributed Stigmergic
  Control for Communications Networks." *JAIR*, 9:317-365, 1998].
- **Collaborative filtering**: User actions (purchases, ratings) serve as implicit pheromones
  that guide other users' discovery [Leskovec, J. et al. "The Dynamics of Viral Marketing."
  *ACM TWEB*, 1(1):5, 2007].
- **Version control**: Git repositories are stigmergic environments — commits, branches, and
  merge patterns guide future development (see `02-git-as-stigmergy.md`).

---

## Stigmergy in Roko's Architecture

Roko implements stigmergy at multiple architectural layers, making it a first-class
coordination primitive rather than an afterthought.

### Layer Mapping

| Layer | Stigmergic Component | Implementation |
|-------|---------------------|----------------|
| L0 Runtime | Engram persistence, decay timers, event emission | `roko-fs` (FileSubstrate JSONL), adaptive clock |
| L1 Framework | Pheromone type system, transport backends | `PheromoneKind` enum, `PheromoneScope` enum, WebSocket/Iroh/ERC-8004 |
| L2 Scaffold | Pheromone-enriched context assembly | Context composer includes ambient pheromone summary |
| L3 Harness | Pheromone-based gate thresholds | Threat pheromone concentration adjusts gate strictness |
| L4 Orchestration | Multi-agent pheromone coordination, morphogenetic specialization | Collective-level pheromone field, reaction-diffusion dynamics |

### The Stigmergic Loop

The complete stigmergic loop in Roko follows this sequence:

```
Agent A acts → deposits pheromone Engram to Substrate
    ↓
Engram propagates (local → Mesh → Global based on scope)
    ↓
Agent B queries Substrate → senses pheromone
    ↓
Scorer rates pheromone intensity and relevance
    ↓
Router selects highest-priority pheromone signal
    ↓
Agent B acts in response → deposits its own pheromone Engram
    ↓
(cycle continues — emergent coordination without direct communication)
```

This loop is isomorphic to Roko's universal cognitive loop (query → score → route → compose →
act → verify → write → react), with pheromone Engrams serving as the coordination substrate.

### Three Knowledge Scopes as Stigmergic Layers

Roko's three-level knowledge architecture maps directly to three stigmergic scopes (see
`05-pheromone-scope.md` for full details):

| Scope | Environment | Persistence | Audience | Example |
|-------|-------------|-------------|----------|---------|
| `Local(SubstrateId)` | Agent's own NeuroStore | Infinite (until GC) | Self only | "I found a bug in module X" |
| `Mesh(CollectiveId)` | Collective's Agent Mesh | Configurable (hours–days) | Collective members | "Module X has a regression" |
| `Global` | Korai chain (public) | Permanent (on-chain) | All agents | "CVE-2026-XXXX affects dependency Y" |

Each scope represents a different stigmergic environment with different persistence
characteristics, audience sizes, and trust levels. Information flows upward through promotion
gates: a local observation can be promoted to Mesh scope after confidence validation, and from
Mesh to Global after collective confirmation.

---

## The Grossman-Stiglitz Paradox and Pheromone Economics

A fundamental challenge in any information-sharing system is the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox:
if information is freely available, no agent has an incentive to incur the cost of producing
it [Grossman, S.J. & Stiglitz, J.E. "On the Impossibility of Informationally Efficient
Markets." *American Economic Review*, 70(3):393-408, 1980].

Roko resolves this paradox through the pheromone system's natural properties:

1. **Decay creates scarcity**: Pheromones decay exponentially. Information that was freely
   sensed yesterday may no longer be available today. Agents that produce fresh signals provide
   genuine value to the collective.

2. **Confirmation extends value**: An agent that confirms another agent's pheromone (by
   depositing the same kind at the same scope) extends the effective half-life. This is a
   cooperative act that benefits the confirming agent (stronger signal for its own decisions)
   and the collective (more persistent shared knowledge).

3. **Reputation tracks contribution**: Agents that consistently produce high-quality pheromones
   (signals that are later confirmed rather than contradicted) accumulate reputation, which
   affects their routing priority and resource allocation.

4. **Domain scoping prevents free-riding**: Pheromones are scoped to specific domains. An
   agent must be active in a domain to sense its pheromones, creating natural communities of
   practice where contributors and consumers overlap.

---

## Research Foundations

The theoretical foundations of stigmergy draw from multiple disciplines:

### Entomology and Ethology

- [Grassé 1959] — Original observation of stigmergy in termite mound construction
- [Wilson, E.O. "The Insect Societies." Belknap Press, 1971] — Comprehensive treatment of
  social insect coordination mechanisms
- [Hölldobler, B. & Wilson, E.O. "The Superorganism." W.W. Norton, 2008] — Superorganism
  theory and multi-level coordination in ant colonies
- [Camazine, S. et al. "Self-Organization in Biological Systems." Princeton University Press,
  2001] — General principles of biological self-organization

### Swarm Intelligence

- [Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz 1999] — *Swarm Intelligence: From Natural to Artificial
  Systems*, the definitive bridge between biological stigmergy and computational applications
- [Dorigo, Maniezzo & Colorni 1996] — Ant Colony Optimization, the first major computational
  application of stigmergy
- [Kennedy, J. & Eberhart, R. "Particle Swarm Optimization." *IEEE ICNN*, 1995] — Related
  swarm intelligence paradigm

### Multi-Agent Systems

- [Parunak 1997] — "Go to the Ant" — engineering principles from biological multi-agent systems
- [Parunak, Brueckner & Sauter 2005] — Digital pheromones for coordination
- [Theraulaz & Bonabeau 1999] — History and formalization of stigmergy

### Distributed Systems

- [Lamport, L. "Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System." *CACM*,
  21(7), 1978] — Foundation for version vectors used in pheromone deduplication across transports
- [Fidge, C.J. "Timestamps in Message-Passing Systems." *ACSC*, 10(1), 1988] — Vector clock
  formalization applied to Engram sequence tracking

---

## Summary

Stigmergy is Roko's primary coordination mechanism because it provides:

1. **Linear scalability** — O(N × M) vs O(N²) for direct communication
2. **Inherent robustness** — no single point of failure, graceful degradation
3. **Temporal decoupling** — producers and consumers operate asynchronously
4. **Minimal agent complexity** — deposit + sense, no knowledge of other agents needed
5. **Natural information economics** — decay, confirmation, and reputation resolve the
   Grossman-Stiglitz paradox

The next sub-docs detail how these principles are instantiated in Roko's digital pheromone
system (`03-digital-pheromones.md`), the specific pheromone types (`04-pheromone-kinds.md`),
scope model (`05-pheromone-scope.md`), and transport layer (`06-agent-mesh-sync.md`).

---

## Related Sub-Docs

- `01-stigmergy-beyond-termites.md` — Stigmergy in non-biological systems
- `02-git-as-stigmergy.md` — Version control as a stigmergic environment
- `03-digital-pheromones.md` — Roko's typed pheromone Engram system
- `07-morphogenetic-specialization.md` — Turing reaction-diffusion for role emergence
- `10-exponential-flywheel.md` — How stigmergy enables superlinear scaling

content.content:
1→# Stigmergy Beyond Termites: Coordination Patterns Across Domains
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L4 Orchestration (coordination theory), with examples touching all layers L0–L4
     4→>
     5→> **Synapse traits**: All six traits appear in the examples below — stigmergy is the universal
     6→> coordination mechanism that the Synapse Architecture implements
     7→>
     8→> **Prerequisites**: `00-stigmergy-theory.md` (core stigmergy definitions)
     9→
    10→
    11→> **Implementation**: Specified
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→## Overview
    16→
    17→Stigmergy — indirect coordination through environmental modification — is not limited to
    18→termite mounds or ant trails. It appears in every domain where agents modify a shared
    19→environment and other agents respond to those modifications. This sub-doc catalogs the diverse
    20→manifestations of stigmergy across biology, human systems, and software, establishing why Roko
    21→adopts it as a **universal coordination primitive** rather than a domain-specific hack.
    22→
    23→The key insight: **any system where work products guide future work is stigmergic.** This
    24→includes open-source development, scientific publishing, market price formation, urban
    25→planning, and neural computation. Roko generalizes these patterns into a single framework of
    26→typed, decaying, scoped Engrams (digital pheromones) that work identically regardless of the
    27→domain.
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## Biological Stigmergy
    32→
    33→### Termite Mound Construction (Grassé 1959)
    34→
    35→The original and canonical example. Termites of the species *Bellicositermes natalensis*
    36→construct mounds up to 5 meters tall containing elaborate chamber networks, ventilation shafts,
    37→fungus gardens, and nurseries — all without any central plan or coordinator.
    38→
    39→Grassé identified the mechanism: each termite's construction behavior is triggered by the
    40→current state of the local structure. A partially built arch "invites" completion. A chamber
    41→of a certain size "invites" a ventilation shaft. The mound itself is the communication medium.
    42→
    43→Key observation: the mound's complexity far exceeds the cognitive capacity of any individual
    44→termite. This is a hallmark of stigmergic systems — **collective output exceeds the sum of
    45→individual capabilities** [Grassé, P.-P. "La Reconstruction du Nid et les Coordinations
    46→Inter-Individuelles chez Bellicositermes Natalensis et Cubitermes sp." *Insectes Sociaux*,
    47→6(1):41-80, 1959].
    48→
    49→### Ant Trail Pheromones (Deneubourg et al. 1990)
    50→
    51→Ants foraging for food deposit pheromone trails on their return path. The trail pheromone is a
    52→volatile chemical that evaporates over time (half-life varies by species: 20 minutes for
    53→*Lasius niger*, several hours for *Atta* leafcutter ants). The concentration of pheromone on a
    54→trail encodes information about the quality and proximity of the food source, because:
    55→
    56→- Ants returning from a closer source traverse the trail more frequently, depositing more
    57→  pheromone per unit time.
    58→- Ants returning from a richer source deposit pheromone at a higher rate (recruitment intensity
    59→  correlates with food quality).
    60→- Pheromone evaporation ensures that trails to depleted sources naturally fade.
    61→
    62→This creates a positive feedback loop: good trails get reinforced, bad trails decay. The
    63→collective converges on optimal foraging paths without any ant computing a shortest-path
    64→algorithm [Deneubourg, J.-L. et al. "The Self-Organizing Exploratory Pattern of the Argentine
    65→Ant." *Journal of Insect Behavior*, 3(2):159-168, 1990].
    66→
    67→**Roko parallel**: Digital pheromones with exponential decay (`intensity(t) = base × e^(-0.693 × elapsed / τ)`) and confirmation reinforcement (effective half-life extends with each independent confirmation). Threat pheromones decay in 2 hours (like volatile ant pheromones), while Wisdom pheromones persist for 24 hours (like stable structural modifications).
    68→
    69→### Honeybee Waggle Dance (Von Frisch 1967)
    70→
    71→While often classified as direct communication, the waggle dance is actually stigmergic in
    72→its systemic effect. A forager returning to the hive performs a dance that encodes the
    73→direction and distance of a food source. But the dance is performed on the comb surface — a
    74→shared environment — and multiple foragers may dance simultaneously, creating a "marketplace"
    75→of competing signals where the colony collectively selects foraging priorities based on the
    76→aggregate dance activity [Von Frisch, K. *The Dance Language and Orientation of Bees*. Belknap
    77→Press, 1967].
    78→
    79→The environment (the dance floor) mediates the coordination. No bee "decides" which food
    80→source the colony should prioritize. The emergent allocation of foragers to food sources arises
    81→from the stigmergic interaction between dances and dancers.
    82→
    83→**Roko parallel**: Multiple agents depositing competing pheromones (e.g., `Opportunity`
    84→pheromones at different scopes). The Router selects the highest-scored pheromone signal,
    85→analogous to a forager choosing which waggle dance to follow.
    86→
    87→### Quorum Sensing in Bacteria (Nealson et al. 1970)
    88→
    89→Bacteria coordinate gene expression through quorum sensing — a stigmergic mechanism where
    90→individual bacteria release signaling molecules (autoinducers) into their environment. When
    91→the local concentration of autoinducers exceeds a threshold (indicating sufficient population
    92→density), all bacteria in the vicinity simultaneously activate specific genes [Nealson, K.H.,
    93→Platt, T. & Hastings, J.W. "Cellular Control of the Synthesis and Activity of the Bacterial
    94→Luminescent System." *Journal of Bacteriology*, 104(1):313-322, 1970].
    95→
    96→Quorum sensing exhibits all three stigmergic conditions:
    97→
    98→1. **Shared environment**: The extracellular medium
    99→2. **Persistent modifications**: Autoinducer molecules accumulate
   100→3. **Stimulus-response coupling**: Threshold concentration triggers coordinated behavior
   101→
   102→**Roko parallel**: Pheromone confirmation mechanics. When multiple agents independently deposit
   103→the same pheromone kind at the same scope, the effective half-life extends:
   104→`τ_effective = τ_base × (1 + confirmations × 0.5)`. This is analogous to quorum sensing — the
   105→pheromone becomes "activated" (long-lived enough to influence collective behavior) only when
   106→enough agents independently confirm it. A single agent's observation is tentative; a
   107→collectively confirmed observation is persistent.
   108→
   109→### Spider Web Construction (Krink & Vollrath 2000)
   110→
   111→Spiders construct webs using a combination of sematectonic and marker-based stigmergy. The
   112→physical structure of the web (thread tension, radial spacing, spiral density) guides
   113→subsequent construction steps, while chemical cues on the silk threads mark territorial
   114→boundaries and prey capture zones [Krink, T. & Vollrath, F. "Analysing Spider Web-Building
   115→Behaviour with Rule-Based Simulations and Genetic Algorithms." *Journal of Theoretical
   116→Biology*, 185(3):321-331, 1997].
   117→
   118→**Roko parallel**: The dual nature of Roko's stigmergic system — sematectonic (code structure
   119→guides agents) and marker-based (explicit pheromone Engrams) — mirrors the spider's dual
   120→signaling strategy.
   121→
   122→---
   123→
   124→## Human Stigmergy
   125→
   126→### Wikipedia as Stigmergy (Elliott 2006)
   127→
   128→Mark Elliott's seminal analysis of Wikipedia identified it as a stigmergic system: editors
   129→modify a shared environment (the wiki), and these modifications guide subsequent edits. An
   130→incomplete article "invites" expansion. A disputed claim "invites" citation. A vandalized page
   131→"invites" reversion. No editor needs to coordinate with other editors directly — the state of
   132→the article itself is the coordination medium [Elliott, M. "Stigmergic Collaboration: A
   133→Theoretical Framework for Mass Collaboration." Ph.D. dissertation, University of Melbourne,
   134→2006].
   135→
   136→Elliott identified four properties that make Wikipedia stigmergic:
   137→
   138→| Property | Wikipedia | Roko |
   139→|----------|-----------|------|
   140→| **Openness** | Anyone can edit | Any agent can deposit pheromones within its scope |
   141→| **Persistence** | Edits persist in revision history | Engrams persist with configurable decay |
   142→| **Self-selection** | Editors choose what to work on based on article state | Agents select tasks based on pheromone gradients |
   143→| **Emergence** | Encyclopedia quality emerges without top-down editorial control | Collective intelligence emerges without centralized coordination |
   144→
   145→### Scientific Publishing as Stigmergy
   146→
   147→The scientific literature is a stigmergic environment:
   148→
   149→1. Researcher A publishes a paper (deposits a "signal" in the shared environment of journals).
   150→2. Researcher B reads the paper and is stimulated to pursue a related question (senses the
   151→   signal and responds).
   152→3. Researcher B publishes a follow-up paper, which stimulates Researchers C, D, E...
   153→4. Citation counts serve as a form of "pheromone concentration" — highly cited papers attract
   154→   more attention and stimulate more follow-up work.
   155→5. Retractions serve as "anti-pheromones" — they repel future researchers from building on
   156→   flawed findings.
   157→
   158→The structure of scientific knowledge emerges from this stigmergic process without any central
   159→planner deciding what research should be done.
   160→
   161→**Roko parallel**: Wisdom pheromones (24-hour half-life) that encode validated insights.
   162→Citation-like reinforcement through the confirmation mechanism. Anti-pheromones through
   163→contradicting deposits (a `Threat` pheromone deposited against a previously trusted `Wisdom`
   164→Engram).
   165→
   166→### Urban Development as Stigmergy (Heylighen 2016)
   167→
   168→Cities grow stigmergically: a new road attracts development along its path; commercial
   169→development attracts residential development; residential density attracts transit investment;
   170→transit attracts more commercial development. No city planner designs this feedback loop — it
   171→emerges from the accumulated modifications to the shared environment (the city itself)
   172→[Heylighen, F. "Stigmergy as a Universal Coordination Mechanism I: Definition and Components."
   173→*Cognitive Systems Research*, 38:4-13, 2016].
   174→
   175→Heylighen formalized stigmergy as a universal coordination mechanism with three components:
   176→
   177→1. **Agent**: An autonomous entity that can perceive and modify the environment
   178→2. **Environment**: A shared medium that persists modifications
   179→3. **Action-perception loop**: Agent perceives environment → acts → modifies environment →
   180→   other agents perceive modified environment
   181→
   182→This formalization applies identically to termite mounds, Wikipedia, and Roko's pheromone
   183→system.
   184→
   185→### Open-Source Software as Stigmergy (Bolici et al. 2009)
   186→
   187→Open-source projects exhibit stigmergy at multiple scales:
   188→
   189→- **Bug reports** are pheromones: they signal where work is needed. High-priority bugs
   190→  attract more developer attention (higher "pheromone intensity").
   191→- **Pull requests** are structural modifications: they change the codebase, creating new
   192→  affordances and constraints for future contributors.
   193→- **Commit logs** are trail pheromones: they record the path taken through the solution space,
   194→  guiding future decisions.
   195→- **CI/CD status badges** are ambient signals: green badges attract feature work, red badges
   196→  attract debugging effort.
   197→
   198→[Bolici, F. et al. "The Challenge of Scalability in Open Source Software: A Stigmergic
   199→Perspective." *AMCIS 2009 Proceedings*, Paper 556, 2009]
   200→
   201→**Roko parallel**: This is precisely the model Roko uses for multi-agent code development.
   202→Coding agents deposit `Pattern` pheromones ("PATTERN trace") in the codebase. Testing agents
   203→sense code changes (sematectonic stigmergy) and respond by writing tests. The CI gate results
   204→serve as `Threat` or `Opportunity` pheromones that guide subsequent agents.
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Computational Stigmergy
   209→
   210→### Ant Colony Optimization (Dorigo et al. 1996)
   211→
   212→The computational formalization of ant foraging stigmergy (see `00-stigmergy-theory.md` for
   213→details). ACO has been applied to:
   214→
   215→| Problem Domain | Reference | Key Result |
   216→|---------------|-----------|------------|
   217→| Travelling Salesman | Dorigo, Maniezzo & Colorni 1996 | Within 2% of optimal for 200-city instances |
   218→| Vehicle Routing | Bullnheimer et al. 1999 | Competitive with best metaheuristics |
   219→| Network Routing | Di Caro & Dorigo 1998 (AntNet) | Adaptive, outperforms OSPF under load |
   220→| Job Scheduling | Merkle et al. 2002 | Near-optimal makespan minimization |
   221→| Protein Folding | Shmygelska & Hoos 2005 | HP lattice model, competitive results |
   222→| Graph Coloring | Costa & Hertz 1997 | Effective for sparse graphs |
   223→
   224→### Particle Swarm Optimization (Kennedy & Eberhart 1995)
   225→
   226→While PSO uses a different mathematical framework than ACO, it shares the stigmergic principle:
   227→each particle's position and velocity encode information that influences the swarm's collective
   228→search behavior. The "personal best" and "global best" positions serve as pheromone-like
   229→attractors in the search space [Kennedy, J. & Eberhart, R. "Particle Swarm Optimization."
   230→*Proceedings of IEEE ICNN*, 4:1942-1948, 1995].
   231→
   232→### Swarm Robotics (Sahin 2005)
   233→
   234→Physical robot swarms use digital pheromones (projected light patterns, RFID tags, or virtual
   235→pheromone fields maintained in a shared server) for coordination:
   236→
   237→- **Foraging**: Robots deposit virtual pheromone trails to guide others to resource locations
   238→- **Construction**: Robots place building blocks that guide subsequent placement decisions
   239→  (sematectonic stigmergy)
   240→- **Formation control**: Robots emit repulsive and attractive pheromones to maintain spacing
   241→
   242→[Sahin, E. "Swarm Robotics: From Sources of Inspiration to Domains of Application." *Swarm
   243→Robotics Workshop*, LNCS 3342, Springer, 2005]
   244→
   245→**Roko parallel**: Multi-agent task allocation via pheromone gradients. An agent that
   246→completes a task deposits an `Opportunity` pheromone near related unfinished tasks, guiding
   247→other agents toward productive work.
   248→
   249→---
   250→
   251→## Stigmergy in Software Engineering
   252→
   253→### Code Smells as Pheromones
   254→
   255→Martin Fowler's concept of "code smells" [Fowler, M. *Refactoring: Improving the Design of
   256→Existing Code*. Addison-Wesley, 1999] is inherently stigmergic. A "smell" is a signal left in
   257→the environment (the codebase) by past development activity that triggers a specific response
   258→(refactoring) in a developer who encounters it.
   259→
   260→| Code Smell | Pheromone Equivalent | Triggered Action |
   261→|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
   262→| Long method | `Pattern` (complexity signal) | Extract method refactoring |
   263→| Feature envy | `Pattern` (coupling signal) | Move method to appropriate class |
   264→| Duplicated code | `Pattern` (redundancy signal) | Extract shared abstraction |
   265→| Dead code | `Anomaly` (staleness signal) | Delete unused code |
   266→| Missing tests | `Opportunity` (coverage gap) | Write tests |
   267→
   268→In Roko, a coding agent can explicitly deposit `Pattern` pheromone Engrams when it detects
   269→code smells, making the stigmergic signaling explicit and typed rather than implicit and
   270→subjective.
   271→
   272→### Niche Construction in Software Ecosystems (Odling-Smee et al. 2003)
   273→
   274→Niche construction theory from evolutionary biology describes how organisms modify their
   275→environment, changing the selection pressures that operate on themselves and their descendants
   276→[Odling-Smee, F.J., Laland, K.N. & Feldman, M.W. *Niche Construction: The Neglected Process
   277→in Evolution*. Princeton University Press, 2003].
   278→
   279→Applied to software agents, niche construction means that **agents modify the codebase in ways
   280→that change the affordances available to future agents**:
   281→
   282→1. Agent A writes a well-documented API module → creates affordances for integration agents.
   283→2. Agent B uses the API to build a feature → validates the API design, deposits `Pattern`
   284→   trace ("this API works well for feature X").
   285→3. Agent C reads the Pattern trace and builds a similar feature using the same API → reinforces
   286→   the niche.
   287→4. The API module becomes a "niche" that attracts further development — a self-reinforcing
   288→   cycle analogous to ecological niche construction.
   289→
   290→This is formalized in Roko through the `AffordanceScore` concept: each agent evaluates the
   291→codebase for affordances (existing code structures that enable new work) and constraints
   292→(code structures that impede work). The balance between affordances and constraints guides
   293→task selection — agents naturally gravitate toward high-affordance niches where productive work
   294→is possible [Gibson, J.J. "The Theory of Affordances." *The Ecological Approach to Visual
   295→Perception*. Lawrence Erlbaum, 1979].
   296→
   297→### Information Foraging Theory (Pirolli & Card 1999)
   298→
   299→Information foraging theory models how people (and agents) navigate information environments by
   300→following "information scent" — cues that indicate the likelihood of finding useful information
   301→along a particular path [Pirolli, P. & Card, S.K. "Information Foraging." *Psychological
   302→Review*, 106(4):643-675, 1999].
   303→
   304→In Roko, information scent maps to pheromone intensity. An agent exploring a codebase follows
   305→the highest-intensity pheromone trails:
   306→
   307→- `Opportunity` pheromones with high intensity → "high information scent" → agent explores
   308→  further in that direction.
   309→- `Threat` pheromones → "danger scent" → agent avoids or prioritizes fixing.
   310→- `Wisdom` pheromones → "knowledge scent" → agent leverages existing insights.
   311→
   312→The information foraging model explains why pheromone decay is essential: without decay, the
   313→environment would become saturated with stale signals, making it impossible for agents to
   314→distinguish fresh, high-quality information from historical noise. Decay creates the
   315→information gradient that makes foraging productive.
   316→
   317→---
   318→
   319→## Generalized Stigmergy: The Domain-Agnostic Pattern
   320→
   321→Roko generalizes stigmergy beyond any single domain. The core abstraction is:
   322→
   323→```
   324→Agent deposits typed Engram → Engram propagates through scope →
   325→Other agent senses Engram → Scorer evaluates → Router selects → Agent acts
   326→```
   327→
   328→This pattern is identical whether the domain is:
   329→
   330→| Domain | "Agent" | "Environment" | "Pheromone" |
   331→|--------|---------|---------------|-------------|
   332→| Software development | Coding agent | Git repository | Pattern traces, test results |
   333→| Research | Research agent | Knowledge base | Citations, findings, hypotheses |
   334→| Operations | Monitoring agent | Infrastructure metrics | Alerts, capacity signals |
   335→| Blockchain (DeFi) | Trading agent | On-chain state | Market signals, arbitrage opportunities |
   336→| Security | Security agent | Vulnerability database | Threat indicators, patch status |
   337→| Data engineering | ETL agent | Data pipeline | Quality metrics, freshness signals |
   338→
   339→The `PheromoneKind` enum (see `04-pheromone-kinds.md`) supports this generalization through
   340→its `Custom(String)` variant, allowing domain-specific pheromone types while preserving the
   341→universal stigmergic infrastructure (decay, confirmation, scoping, routing).
   342→
   343→---
   344→
   345→## The Constructal Law Connection (Bejan 1997)
   346→
   347→Adrian Bejan's Constructal Law states that "for a finite-size flow system to persist in time,
   348→it must evolve such that it provides easier and easier access to the currents that flow through
   349→it" [Bejan, A. "Constructal-Theory Network of Conducting Paths for Cooling a Heat Generating
   350→Volume." *International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer*, 40(4):799-816, 1997].
   351→
   352→Applied to stigmergic systems, this predicts that pheromone networks will naturally evolve
   353→toward dendritic (tree-like) hierarchies — not flat topologies. The most efficient knowledge
   354→distribution follows a branching pattern:
   355→
   356→1. **Trunk**: High-bandwidth, high-persistence channels (Global scope on Korai chain) carrying
   357→   universally relevant signals.
   358→2. **Branches**: Medium-bandwidth channels (Mesh scope within Collectives) carrying
   359→   domain-specific signals.
   360→3. **Leaves**: Low-bandwidth, ephemeral channels (Local scope within individual agents)
   361→   carrying highly specific, short-lived signals.
   362→
   363→Roko's three-scope pheromone system (`Local` → `Mesh` → `Global`) implements this constructal
   364→hierarchy. Knowledge flows upward through promotion gates (increasing persistence and audience)
   365→and downward through query mechanisms (increasing specificity and relevance).
   366→
   367→---
   368→
   369→## Self-Organized Criticality and Stigmergy (Bak et al. 1987)
   370→
   371→Self-organized criticality (SOC) describes systems that naturally evolve to a critical state
   372→where small perturbations can trigger cascading events of all sizes, following a power-law
   373→distribution [Bak, P., Tang, C. & Wiesenfeld, K. "Self-Organized Criticality: An Explanation
   374→of the 1/f Noise." *Physical Review Letters*, 59(4):381-384, 1987].
   375→
   376→Stigmergic systems exhibit SOC properties:
   377→
   378→- Small pheromone deposits usually have local effects (most signals decay without triggering
   379→  cascade behavior).
   380→- Occasionally, a pheromone deposit triggers a chain reaction: one agent's signal causes
   381→  another agent to act, which produces more signals, which trigger more agents.
   382→- The distribution of cascade sizes follows a power law — many small events, few large ones.
   383→
   384→Roko's adaptive pheromone system is designed to operate near this critical state. The
   385→exponential decay rate prevents supercritical cascades (runaway positive feedback), while the
   386→confirmation mechanism prevents subcritical damping (useful signals fading before they can
   387→influence the collective).
   388→
   389→The target operating point is at the "edge of chaos" — the boundary between ordered and
   390→chaotic behavior where computational capacity is maximized [Kauffman, S. *The Origins of
   391→Order*. Oxford University Press, 1993]. Roko's adaptive gate thresholds (see the `roko-gate`
   392→crate) serve as the tuning mechanism that keeps the system near criticality.
   393→
   394→---
   395→
   396→## Summary: Why Every Domain Is Stigmergic
   397→
   398→The examples in this sub-doc demonstrate a universal pattern:
   399→
   400→1. **Agents modify a shared environment** (codebase, knowledge base, market, wiki, city)
   401→2. **Modifications persist** (with varying decay rates)
   402→3. **Other agents respond to modifications** (closing the loop)
   403→4. **Complex global behavior emerges** from simple local interactions
   404→
   405→Roko's contribution is to make this pattern **explicit, typed, and programmable** through the
   406→Synapse Architecture. Instead of relying on implicit stigmergy (code structure happens to guide
   407→agents), Roko adds explicit stigmergy (typed pheromone Engrams with controlled decay,
   408→confirmation, and scoping) on top. Both forms coexist and reinforce each other.
   409→
   410→The next sub-doc (`02-git-as-stigmergy.md`) examines the most important stigmergic environment
   411→for software development agents: the Git repository.
   412→
   413→---
   414→
   415→## References
   416→
   417→- [Bak, Tang & Wiesenfeld 1987] Self-Organized Criticality, *Physical Review Letters*
   418→- [Bejan 1997] Constructal Law, *Int. J. Heat and Mass Transfer*
   419→- [Bolici et al. 2009] Scalability in OSS via stigmergy, *AMCIS*
   420→- [Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz 1999] *Swarm Intelligence*, Oxford University Press
   421→- [Deneubourg et al. 1990] Argentine ant self-organization, *J. Insect Behavior*
   422→- [Di Caro & Dorigo 1998] AntNet routing, *JAIR*
   423→- [Dorigo, Maniezzo & Colorni 1996] Ant Colony Optimization, *IEEE SMC-B*
   424→- [Elliott 2006] Stigmergic Collaboration, University of Melbourne
   425→- [Fowler 1999] *Refactoring*, Addison-Wesley
   426→- [Gibson 1979] Affordance theory, *Ecological Approach to Visual Perception*
   427→- [Grassé 1959] Termite mound stigmergy, *Insectes Sociaux*
   428→- [Heylighen 2016] Universal Coordination Mechanism, *Cognitive Systems Research*
   429→- [Hölldobler & Wilson 2008] *The Superorganism*, W.W. Norton
   430→- [Kauffman 1993] *The Origins of Order*, Oxford University Press
   431→- [Kennedy & Eberhart 1995] Particle Swarm Optimization, *IEEE ICNN*
   432→- [Nealson, Platt & Hastings 1970] Quorum sensing, *J. Bacteriology*
   433→- [Odling-Smee, Laland & Feldman 2003] *Niche Construction*, Princeton University Press
   434→- [Parunak 1997] Engineering from natural MAS, *Ann. Oper. Res.*
   435→- [Parunak, Brueckner & Sauter 2005] Digital pheromones, *E4MAS*
   436→- [Pirolli & Card 1999] Information Foraging, *Psychological Review*
   437→- [Sahin 2005] Swarm Robotics, *SR Workshop*
   438→- [Von Frisch 1967] *The Dance Language and Orientation of Bees*, Belknap Press
   439→
