    17→## Overview
    18→
    19→The Agent Mesh (renamed from "Styx" in the legacy architecture) is Roko's peer-to-peer
    20→connectivity layer for Mesh-scope pheromone propagation, collective knowledge sharing, and
    21→morphogenetic coordination signals. It provides transport between agents in a Collective
    22→(renamed from "Clade") without requiring a centralized message broker.
    23→
    24→The Agent Mesh supports three co-equal transport mechanisms:
    25→
    26→| Transport | Technology | Latency | NAT Traversal | Encryption | Best For |
    27→|-----------|-----------|---------|---------------|------------|----------|
    28→| **WebSocket** | Standard WSS | ~50ms | N/A (outbound only) | TLS 1.3 | Always-available relay, store-and-forward |
    29→| **Iroh** | QUIC + ed25519 | ~10ms LAN, ~100ms WAN | Hole-punching + relay fallback | QUIC TLS 1.3 | Direct P2P, gossip pub/sub, blob transfer |
    30→| **ERC-8004** | On-chain registry | ~minutes | N/A | N/A | Agent discovery, service endpoint resolution |
    31→
    32→These transports are complementary, not competing. The recommended configuration uses all three:
    33→Iroh for direct agent-to-agent communication, WebSocket as a fallback relay with store-and-
    34→forward for offline agents, and ERC-8004 for discovery and identity binding.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## The Dual-Transport Architecture
    39→
    40→### Four Valid Transport Combinations
    41→
    42→| WebSocket | Iroh | Behavior |
    43→|-----------|------|----------|
    44→| off | off | Local-only agent. No Mesh sync, no pheromones, no collective coordination. ~95% core capability via local NeuroStore. Valid for development, testing, or isolated operation. |
    45→| on | off | Classic relay model. All 16 services multiplexed over a single outbound WebSocket. Store-and-forward for offline agents (7-day TTL). Simple, reliable. |
    46→| off | on | Pure P2P. Collective sync via direct QUIC streams, pheromones via gossip, knowledge via content-addressed blobs. No store-and-forward for offline agents. |
    47→| **on** | **on** | **Best of both (recommended).** Iroh for direct peer communication (lower latency, no relay hop). WebSocket for store-and-forward, persistence, and services that require a server. Dedup via version vectors when same message arrives via both. |
    48→
    49→### EventFabric as Integration Point
    50→
    51→Regardless of transport, all messages converge on the EventFabric — Roko's internal event bus.
    52→A pheromone arriving via WebSocket and the same pheromone arriving via Iroh produce identical
    53→events. Consumers (NeuroStore ingestion, context assembly, morphogenetic coordinator) never
    54→know which transport delivered a message.
    55→
    56→Deduplication happens at the ingestion boundary via version vectors (`{agent_id → last_seen_seq}`). If a pheromone arrives via both WebSocket and Iroh, the second arrival is silently dropped.
    57→
    58→---
    59→
    60→## WebSocket Transport
    61→
    62→### The Outbound WebSocket Model
    63→
    64→Every agent that enables Agent Mesh maintains ONE persistent outbound WebSocket connection.
    65→This connection works from behind any NAT, firewall, or restrictive network — it is a standard
    66→outbound HTTPS connection on port 443. No inbound ports, no tunnels, no port forwarding.
    67→
    68→The WebSocket connection serves as a bidirectional channel for ALL Mesh traffic: collective
    69→sync, pheromone updates, knowledge exchange, event relay, and TUI event forwarding. This
    70→multiplexed design minimizes connection overhead while supporting the full range of Mesh
    71→services.
    72→
    73→### Connection Registry
    74→
    75→The Agent Mesh server maintains an in-memory registry of connected agents, indexed by Collective:
    76→
    77→```rust
    78→/// In-memory registry of connected agents.
    79→/// When Agent A pushes a delta, the Mesh server looks up all peers
    80→/// (same Collective, different agent) and pushes to their WebSocket connections.
    81→pub struct ConnectionRegistry {
    82→    /// Map from CollectiveId to list of connected agents.
    83→    connections: DashMap<CollectiveId, Vec<ConnectedAgent>>,
    84→}
    85→
    86→impl ConnectionRegistry {
    87→    /// Route a pheromone delta to all Collective members except the sender.
    88→    pub async fn route_pheromone(
    89→        &self,
    90→        sender_id: &AgentId,
    91→        collective_id: &CollectiveId,
    92→        delta: PheromoneDelta,
    93→    ) {
    94→        if let Some(peers) = self.connections.get(collective_id) {
    95→            for peer in peers.iter() {
    96→                if peer.agent_id != *sender_id {
    97→                    let _ = peer.tx.send(MeshMessage::PheromoneSync {
    98→                        from: sender_id.clone(),
    99→                        entries: delta.entries.clone(),
   100→                    }).await;
   101→                }
   102→            }
   103→        }
   104→    }
   105→}
   106→```
   107→
   108→### Store-and-Forward
   109→
   110→If an agent is offline when a pheromone delta is pushed, the Mesh server stores it in its
   111→persistence layer. When the agent reconnects, it receives all pending deltas in order. Pending
   112→deltas expire after a configurable TTL (default: 7 days).
   113→
   114→This is critical for asynchronous coordination: an agent that runs overnight should find all
   115→pheromone signals deposited by daytime agents when it boots in the morning.
   116→
   117→### WebSocket message types
   118→
   119→| Message Type | Direction | Content |
   120→|-------------|-----------|---------|
   121→| `PheromoneSync` | Server -> Agent | Batch of pheromone Engrams from Collective peers |
   122→| `PheromoneImmediate` | Server -> Agent | High-priority pheromone (Threat, high-intensity Anomaly) |
   123→| `PheromoneDelta` | Agent -> Server | New pheromone deposits for relay to Collective |
   124→| `KnowledgeSync` | Bidirectional | NeuroStore entries promoted to Mesh scope |
   125→| `MorphogeneticBroadcast` | Agent -> Server -> Peers | Role vector and specialization signals |
   126→| `VersionVector` | Agent -> Server | Sequence numbers for dedup and delta computation |
   127→| `Heartbeat` | Agent -> Server | Liveness signal (every 30s) |
   128→
   129→### Message ordering and priority
   130→
   131→WebSocket messages are ordered by a two-tier priority queue at the sender. High-priority
   132→messages (Threat pheromones, role conflict alerts) preempt batched sync messages.
   133→
   134→```rust
   135→/// Priority levels for outbound WebSocket messages.
   136→/// Lower numeric value = higher priority = sent first.
   137→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]
   138→#[repr(u8)]
   139→pub enum MeshPriority {
   140→    /// Threat pheromones, role conflict alerts, niche vacancy alerts.
   141→    /// Sent immediately, bypassing the batch interval.
   142→    Critical = 0,
   143→    /// PheromoneImmediate (intensity > immediate_threshold).
   144→    High = 1,
   145→    /// Standard batched sync: PheromoneSync, KnowledgeSync, MorphogeneticBroadcast.
   146→    Normal = 2,
   147→    /// Heartbeat, VersionVector exchanges, telemetry.
   148→    Low = 3,
   149→}
   150→
   151→/// Outbound message queue with priority ordering.
   152→///
   153→/// Uses a `BinaryHeap` keyed on `(priority, sequence)` to maintain
   154→/// FIFO order within the same priority level.
   155→///
   156→/// # Flow control
   157→///
   158→/// When the queue exceeds `max_pending` messages, the oldest `Low`
   159→/// priority messages are dropped first, then `Normal`. `Critical` and
   160→/// `High` messages are never dropped — if the queue is full of critical
   161→/// messages, backpressure propagates to the caller via `try_send`.
   162→pub struct PriorityOutbox {
   163→    queue: BinaryHeap<Reverse<(MeshPriority, u64, MeshMessage)>>,
   164→    next_seq: u64,
   165→    /// Maximum pending messages before flow control kicks in.
   166→    /// Default: 1024. Range: [64, 65536].
   167→    max_pending: usize,
   168→}
   169→
   170→impl PriorityOutbox {
   171→    /// Enqueue a message. Returns `Err(QueueFull)` if the queue is at capacity
   172→    /// and no lower-priority message can be evicted.
   173→    pub fn enqueue(
   174→        &mut self,
   175→        priority: MeshPriority,
   176→        msg: MeshMessage,
   177→    ) -> Result<(), QueueFull> {
   178→        if self.queue.len() >= self.max_pending {
   179→            // Try to evict lowest-priority message
   180→            if !self.evict_lowest(priority) {
   181→                return Err(QueueFull);
   182→            }
   183→        }
   184→        let seq = self.next_seq;
   185→        self.next_seq += 1;
   186→        self.queue.push(Reverse((priority, seq, msg)));
   187→        Ok(())
   188→    }
   189→}
   190→```
   191→
   192→**Ordering guarantees**: Messages within the same priority level are delivered in FIFO order
   193→(sequence number tiebreak). Across priority levels, higher-priority messages are sent first.
   194→The WebSocket transport itself is ordered (TCP), so server-side delivery order matches the
   195→sender's priority queue order.
   196→
   197→**Flow control on overflow**: When the outbox hits `max_pending`, the sender evicts the oldest
   198→message at the lowest populated priority level. If only `Critical` messages remain, the
   199→`enqueue` call returns `Err(QueueFull)` and the caller must decide whether to block or drop.
   200→In practice, this only happens during sustained network partitions where the WebSocket write
   201→buffer is full.
   202→
   203→### Full message envelope schema
   204→
   205→Every message on the wire (both WebSocket and Iroh) uses a common envelope:
   206→
   207→```rust
   208→/// Wire-format envelope for all Mesh messages.
   209→///
   210→/// Serialized with postcard (compact binary). The envelope wraps every
   211→/// message type and provides the fields needed for routing, dedup,
   212→/// authentication, and priority handling.
   213→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   214→pub struct MeshEnvelope {
   215→    /// Protocol version. Current: 1. Receivers reject unknown versions.
   216→    pub version: u8,
   217→
   218→    /// Unique message ID (UUIDv7 for time-ordering).
   219→    pub message_id: [u8; 16],
   220→
   221→    /// Sender's agent ID.
   222→    pub sender: AgentId,
   223→
   224→    /// Sender's Collective ID.
   225→    pub collective_id: CollectiveId,
   226→
   227→    /// Monotonically increasing sequence number from this sender.
   228→    /// Used by version vectors for dedup.
   229→    pub seq: u64,
   230→
   231→    /// Message priority (mapped from MeshPriority enum).
   232→    pub priority: u8,
   233→
   234→    /// Unix timestamp in milliseconds when the message was created.
   235→    pub timestamp_ms: u64,
   236→
   237→    /// Domain this message belongs to (for topic routing).
   238→    /// Empty string means "all domains" (e.g., heartbeats).
   239→    pub domain: String,
   240→
   241→    /// The payload, tagged by type.
   242→    pub payload: MeshPayload,
   243→
   244→    /// Ed25519 signature over (version || message_id || sender || seq || payload_hash).
   245→    /// Verified by receivers before processing.
   246→    pub signature: [u8; 64],
   247→}
   248→
   249→/// Tagged union of all possible message payloads.
   250→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   251→pub enum MeshPayload {
   252→    PheromoneDelta(Vec<PheromoneEntry>),
   253→    PheromoneImmediate(PheromoneEntry),
   254→    KnowledgeSync(Vec<KnowledgeEntry>),
   255→    MorphogeneticBroadcast(MorphogeneticPheromone),
   256→    NicheVacancy(NicheVacancy),
   257→    RoleConflict(RoleConflict),
   258→    VersionVector(HashMap<AgentId, u64>),
   259→    Heartbeat { uptime_secs: u64, load: f32 },
   260→}
   261→```
   262→
   263→---
   264→
   265→## Iroh Transport
   266→
   267→### What Is Iroh?
   268→
   269→Iroh (from `n0-computer/iroh`) is a Rust library providing QUIC-based connections dialed by
   270→public key, with NAT traversal and relay fallback. It offers three sub-protocols relevant to
   271→Roko:
   272→
   273→1. **Direct connections**: Dial any agent by its ed25519 public key (`NodeId`). Iroh handles
   274→   address resolution, NAT hole-punching (~70% direct connection success rate), and relay
   275→   fallback.
   276→2. **iroh-gossip**: HyParView + PlumTree pub/sub for efficient multi-agent broadcast. Used for
   277→   pheromone propagation across the Collective.
   278→3. **iroh-blobs**: Content-addressed blob transfer using BLAKE3 hashing. Used for knowledge
   279→   bundle exchange.
   280→
   281→### Connection lifecycle
   282→
   283→1. **Bind**: `Endpoint::builder()` with secret key, ALPN identifier (`roko/mesh/1`), and
   284→   relay configuration. Spawns background tasks for relay connection and hole-punching.
   285→2. **Publish**: Agent publishes its addressing information via pkarr (DNS-over-HTTPS) so peers
   286→   can resolve its `NodeId` to a network address.
   287→3. **Accept**: Incoming connections are screened by Collective membership -- only agents
   288→   registered in the same Collective (verified via ERC-8004 registry or local config) are
   289→   accepted.
   290→4. **Connect**: Outbound connections dial peers by `NodeId`. Iroh resolves via pkarr/DNS,
   291→   attempts direct connection, falls back to relay.
   292→5. **Streams**: Bidirectional QUIC streams for collective sync. One long-lived stream per
   293→   peer, pooled rather than per-message.
   294→6. **Gossip**: Joins topic-based gossip channels for pheromone propagation.
   295→7. **Shutdown**: Close connections, stop accept loop, unpublish from pkarr. 5-second graceful
   296→   timeout.
   297→
   298→### Automatic relay fallback
   299→
   300→When direct QUIC hole-punching fails (~30% of connections, primarily symmetric NAT), Iroh
   301→falls back to relay servers automatically. The fallback is transparent to application code.
   302→
   303→```rust
   304→/// Iroh relay configuration.
   305→///
   306→/// The relay serves two purposes:
   307→/// 1. STUN-like address discovery (always used, even for direct connections)
   308→/// 2. Traffic relay when direct connection fails
   309→///
   310→/// Cost model: relay traffic is metered. Each relayed byte counts against
   311→/// the mesh budget. Direct connections have zero relay cost.
   312→pub struct IrohRelayConfig {
   313→    /// Relay mode selection.
   314→    /// - "default": Use n0's public relay network (free tier: 1GB/month)
   315→    /// - "custom": Use a self-hosted relay at `relay_url`
   316→    /// - "disabled": No relay. Direct connections only (LAN-only operation)
   317→    pub mode: RelayMode,
   318→
   319→    /// URL of self-hosted relay server.
   320→    /// Only used when mode = "custom".
   321→    /// Example: "https://relay.my-mesh.example.com"
   322→    pub relay_url: Option<String>,
   323→
   324→    /// Maximum relay bandwidth per agent per day (bytes).
   325→    /// Default: 100 MB (104_857_600). Range: [1_048_576, 10_737_418_240].
   326→    /// Prevents runaway relay costs from sustained fallback.
   327→    pub max_relay_bytes_per_day: u64,
   328→
   329→    /// Connection timeout before declaring relay fallback.
   330→    /// Default: 5 seconds. Range: [1, 30] seconds.
   331→    pub direct_connect_timeout: Duration,
   332→}
   333→```
   334→
   335→**Fallback sequence**: Iroh attempts direct connection first (UDP hole-punch via STUN). If no
   336→response arrives within `direct_connect_timeout` (default: 5s), traffic routes through the
   337→relay. The switch is per-connection, not global -- an agent can have direct connections to
   338→some peers and relayed connections to others simultaneously.
   339→
   340→**Cost model**: Relayed traffic counts against `max_relay_bytes_per_day`. When the limit is
   341→reached, new relay connections are refused (direct-only or no connection). The daily counter
   342→resets at midnight UTC. Pheromone messages are small (~200 bytes each), so 100 MB supports
   343→roughly 500,000 pheromone exchanges per day through the relay -- well above normal operation.
   344→
   345→### Pheromone Propagation via iroh-gossip
   346→
   347→Each `(domain, regime)` pair gets a gossip topic. Topic IDs are deterministic:
   348→
   349→```
   350→TopicId = blake3::hash(format!("roko/pheromone/{domain}/{regime}"))
   351→```
   352→
   353→The topic set is bounded: domains × regimes ≈ 50 topics maximum. Agents subscribe only to
   354→topics for domains they operate in, preventing unnecessary message delivery.
   355→
   356→`PheromoneDeposit` messages are serialized (postcard encoding) and broadcast via
   357→`sender.broadcast()`. Decay is computed locally by each agent based on the deposit timestamp —
   358→no coordination needed. The existing exponential decay formula applies unchanged.
   359→
   360→When gossip receivers fall behind (`Event::Lagged`), the message is dropped and logged at WARN
   361→level. Pheromone loss is acceptable -- signals are fuzzy by nature, and a lost pheromone will
   362→likely be re-deposited or become irrelevant before its absence matters.
   363→
   364→**Domain list management**: Each agent subscribes to gossip topics based on its configured
   365→domains. The domain list is set at startup from `roko.toml` and can change at runtime when a
   366→domain plugin is loaded or unloaded.
   367→
   368→```rust
   369→/// Manages gossip topic subscriptions for an agent.
   370→///
   371→/// Topic IDs are deterministic: `blake3(format!("roko/pheromone/{domain}/{regime}"))`.
   372→/// When the agent's domain list changes, the manager subscribes to new topics
   373→/// and unsubscribes from removed ones.
   374→pub struct GossipTopicManager {
   375→    /// Currently subscribed topics, keyed by (domain, regime).
   376→    active_topics: HashMap<(String, String), TopicSubscription>,
   377→
   378→    /// Domains this agent operates in. Set from config, updated at runtime.
   379→    domains: Vec<String>,
   380→
   381→    /// Known regimes per domain. Updated when regime-change pheromones arrive.
   382→    regimes: HashMap<String, String>,
   383→}
   384→
   385→impl GossipTopicManager {
   386→    /// Recompute subscriptions after a domain or regime change.
   387→    ///
   388→    /// Subscribes to new (domain, regime) pairs. Unsubscribes from
   389→    /// pairs no longer in the active set. Existing subscriptions
   390→    /// for unchanged pairs are left alone (no reconnect churn).
   391→    pub fn reconcile(&mut self, endpoint: &Endpoint) -> Result<(), MeshError> {
   392→        let desired: HashSet<(String, String)> = self.domains.iter()
   393→            .flat_map(|d| {
   394→                let regime = self.regimes.get(d).cloned()
   395→                    .unwrap_or_else(|| "default".into());
   396→                vec![(d.clone(), regime)]
   397→            })
   398→            .collect();
   399→
   400→        // Unsubscribe from removed topics
   401→        self.active_topics.retain(|key, sub| {
   402→            if !desired.contains(key) {
   403→                sub.leave();
   404→                false
   405→            } else {
   406→                true
   407→            }
   408→        });
   409→
   410→        // Subscribe to new topics
   411→        for key in &desired {
   412→            if !self.active_topics.contains_key(key) {
   413→                let topic_id = Self::topic_id(&key.0, &key.1);
   414→                let sub = endpoint.join_topic(topic_id)?;
   415→                self.active_topics.insert(key.clone(), sub);
   416→            }
   417→        }
   418→        Ok(())
   419→    }
   420→
   421→    fn topic_id(domain: &str, regime: &str) -> TopicId {
   422→        let input = format!("roko/pheromone/{domain}/{regime}");
   423→        TopicId::from(blake3::hash(input.as_bytes()))
   424→    }
   425→}
   426→```
   427→
   428→**Regime detection**: When the Collective detects a regime change (via regime-change pheromones
   429→from `roko-conductor`), the topic manager creates a new topic for the new regime and begins
   430→a 60-second overlap period where the agent subscribes to both old and new regime topics. After
   431→the overlap, the old topic subscription is dropped. This prevents message loss during
   432→transitions.
   433→
   434→**Topic lifecycle**: Topics are created on first subscription and garbage-collected when no
   435→agents remain subscribed. The underlying HyParView protocol handles peer membership -- when
   436→the last subscriber leaves, the topic becomes dormant. Rejoining a dormant topic is
   437→indistinguishable from joining a new one.
   438→
   439→### Knowledge Exchange via iroh-blobs
   440→
   441→For large knowledge artifacts (NeuroStore entries promoted to Mesh scope, knowledge backups),
   442→iroh-blobs provides content-addressed transfer:
   443→
   444→1. Sending agent adds the knowledge bundle to its local iroh-blobs store
   445→2. The BLAKE3 hash is announced via gossip on a `roko/knowledge/{collective_id}` topic
   446→3. Receiving agents download the blob on-demand using the hash
   447→4. BLAKE3's BAO (Authenticated Ordering) provides incremental verification — the transfer
   448→   is verified as it streams, not all-or-nothing
   449→
   450→### Identity Binding
   451→
   452→Each agent has a separate ed25519 keypair for Iroh, distinct from any wallet keys:
   453→
   454→- **Generation**: On first boot, `iroh::SecretKey::generate()` creates a new keypair
   455→- **Persistence**: Secret key stored at `{config.iroh.secret_key_path}` with 0o600 permissions
   456→- **NodeId**: The public key is the agent's stable Iroh identity
   457→- **Registration**: NodeId stored in ERC-8004 `serviceEndpoints` for discovery:
   458→  ```json
   459→  {
   460→    "serviceEndpoints": {
   461→      "ws": "wss://mesh.roko.dev/v1/mesh/ws",
   462→      "iroh": "<base32-encoded-node-id>"
   463→    }
   464→  }
   465→  ```
   466→
   467→---
   468→
   469→## ERC-8004: Agent Discovery
   470→
   471→ERC-8004 (Agent Cards) provides on-chain service discovery for agents. Each registered agent
   472→publishes an Agent Card containing:
   473→
   474→| Field | Description |
   475→|-------|-------------|
   476→| `agentId` | On-chain agent identifier |
   477→| `operator` | Operator address (for Collective discovery) |
   478→| `capabilities` | Bitmask of agent capabilities |
   479→| `serviceEndpoints` | WebSocket URL, Iroh NodeId, other transport addresses |
   480→| `reputation` | Current reputation scores per domain |
   481→| `domains` | Domains the agent operates in |
   482→
   483→### Discovery Scopes
   484→
   485→Three discovery scopes, from local to global:
   486→
   487→#### Scope 1: Same LAN (Zero-Config)
   488→
   489→Iroh's `MdnsAddressLookup` uses mDNS/DNS-SD to discover peers on the local network. No
   490→configuration needed. When two agents run on the same machine or same LAN, they discover each
   491→other automatically.
   492→
   493→#### Scope 2: Same Collective (Cross-Network)
   494→
   495→1. Query ERC-8004 registry: `getAgentsByOperator(operator_address)` → all agents for this
   496→   operator
   497→2. Extract Iroh `NodeId` from each agent's `serviceEndpoints`
   498→3. Feed NodeIds into Iroh's address resolution chain
   499→4. Connect directly or via relay
   500→
   501→Discovery frequency: on boot, on new agent registration (event listener), and periodically
   502→(default: every 300 seconds).
   503→
   504→#### Scope 3: Cross-Collective (Strangers)
   505→
   506→Cross-collective discovery requires an index:
   507→
   508→1. **ERC-8004 browse**: Query the on-chain registry for agents filtered by capability,
   509→   reputation, or domain. Public, permissionless directory.
   510→2. **Semantic search**: Query the knowledge layer for semantic matches. Results include
   511→   publisher NodeId. Knowledge exchange via Iroh after initial discovery.
   512→3. **Gossip bootstrap**: Connect to well-reputed peers from ERC-8004, join domain-specific
   513→   gossip topics, discover additional peers through HyParView peer sampling.
   514→
   515→Cross-collective discovery is off by default (`config.discovery.cross_collective_enabled = false`).
   516→
   517→### Caching strategy and stale data handling
   518→
   519→ERC-8004 queries hit an on-chain registry, which is expensive (gas for writes, RPC latency for
   520→reads). The discovery layer caches Agent Card data locally with a TTL-based invalidation
   521→strategy.
   522→
   523→```rust
   524→/// Local cache for ERC-8004 Agent Card data.
   525→///
   526→/// Reduces on-chain queries to the poll interval (default: 300s).
   527→/// Stale entries are usable but flagged — an agent whose card hasn't
   528→/// been refreshed may have changed endpoints or gone offline.
   529→pub struct AgentCardCache {
   530→    /// Cached cards, keyed by agent ID.
   531→    cards: HashMap<AgentId, CachedCard>,
   532→
   533→    /// How long a cached card is considered fresh.
   534→    /// Default: 300 seconds (matches poll interval).
   535→    /// Range: [60, 3600] seconds.
   536→    pub fresh_ttl: Duration,
   537→
   538→    /// How long a stale card is kept before eviction.
   539→    /// A stale card is still returned (with a `stale` flag) but triggers
   540→    /// an async refresh. Default: 3600 seconds. Range: [300, 86400].
   541→    pub stale_ttl: Duration,
   542→
   543→    /// Maximum cached entries. LRU eviction beyond this limit.
   544→    /// Default: 1000. Range: [100, 100_000].
   545→    pub max_entries: usize,
   546→}
   547→
   548→struct CachedCard {
   549→    card: AgentCard,
   550→    fetched_at: Instant,
   551→}
   552→
   553→impl AgentCardCache {
   554→    /// Look up an agent's card.
   555→    ///
   556→    /// Returns `Fresh(card)` if within fresh_ttl.
   557→    /// Returns `Stale(card)` if within stale_ttl (triggers async refresh).
   558→    /// Returns `Miss` if not cached or beyond stale_ttl.
   559→    pub fn get(&self, agent_id: &AgentId) -> CacheLookup {
   560→        match self.cards.get(agent_id) {
   561→            Some(entry) => {
   562→                let age = entry.fetched_at.elapsed();
   563→                if age < self.fresh_ttl {
   564→                    CacheLookup::Fresh(entry.card.clone())
   565→                } else if age < self.stale_ttl {
   566→                    CacheLookup::Stale(entry.card.clone())
   567→                } else {
   568→                    CacheLookup::Miss
   569→                }
   570→            }
   571→            None => CacheLookup::Miss,
   572→        }
   573→    }
   574→}
   575→```
   576→
   577→**Stale data handling**: When a stale card is returned, the caller proceeds with the cached
   578→endpoints while an async refresh runs in the background. If the refresh reveals changed
   579→endpoints (e.g., new Iroh NodeId), existing connections to the old endpoints are drained
   580→gracefully (5-second timeout) before switching. If the refresh fails (RPC error), the stale
   581→card remains in use until its `stale_ttl` expires.
   582→
   583→**Event-driven invalidation**: In addition to polling, the discovery layer listens for
   584→ERC-8004 `AgentUpdated` and `AgentRemoved` events via an Ethereum event subscription. These
   585→events invalidate the cache entry immediately, avoiding stale data between poll intervals.
   586→
   587→---
   588→
   589→## Service-to-Transport Mapping
   590→
   591→| Service | WebSocket | Iroh | Notes |
   592→|---------|-----------|------|-------|
   593→| Collective sync | Store-and-forward for offline agents | Direct QUIC streams | Iroh preferred, WS fallback |
   594→| Pheromone propagation | Central fan-out from Mesh server | iroh-gossip per topic | Gossip scales better |
   595→| Knowledge exchange | Relay | iroh-blobs (content-addressed) | Iroh preferred |
   596→| Bloom oracle | Server-side cache | Gossip broadcast | Iroh for Collective, WS for ecosystem |
   597→| Causal graph federation | Relay | Gossip | Iroh preferred |
   598→| Event relay (→ TUI) | WS relay | Direct stream | Iroh if TUI is local |
   599→| Persistence backup | WS to server | N/A (requires server) | **WS only** |
   600→| Semantic search | WS to server | N/A (requires vector DB) | **WS only** |
   601→
   602→---
   603→
   604→## Failure Modes and Graceful Degradation
   605→
   606→| Scenario | Behavior |
   607→|----------|----------|
   608→| Iroh endpoint fails to bind | Log warning, disable Iroh, operate WS-only |
   609→| Iroh relay unreachable | Direct connections only; LAN peers still reachable via mDNS |
   610→| Peer unreachable via Iroh | Route via WS for store-and-forward |
   611→| WS relay unreachable | Operate Iroh-only; no persistence backup or semantic search |
   612→| Both transports down | Local-only mode (~95% capability); local NeuroStore operates independently |
   613→| Gossip `Event::Lagged` | Pheromone updates may be lost; acceptable (fuzzy signals) |
   614→| Delta via both transports | Dedup via version vector; second arrival is no-op |
   615→
   616→### Graceful Degradation Hierarchy
   617→
   618→```
   619→Both transports active    → full capability
   620→Iroh only                 → ~85% (sync + pheromones work; no persistence/search)
   621→WS only                   → ~95% (all services via relay; higher latency)
   622→Neither                   → ~95% of core (local NeuroStore operates independently)
   623→```
   624→
   625→The ~95% local-only capability reflects a fundamental design principle: **the Agent Mesh is an
   626→accelerator, not a requirement.** An agent with `mesh.enabled: false` operates on its local
   627→NeuroStore, runs the full cognitive loop (query → score → route → compose → act → verify →
   628→write → react), and produces useful work. What it loses is cross-agent intelligence — valuable
   629→for coordination but not essential for individual agent operation.
   630→
   631→---
   632→
   633→## Security Model
   634→
   635→### Transport-Level Security
   636→
   637→| Transport | Authentication | Encryption | Integrity |
   638→|-----------|---------------|------------|-----------|
   639→| WebSocket | API key or x402 wallet signature linked to ERC-8004 | TLS 1.3 | TLS |
   640→| Iroh | Mutual ed25519 authentication during QUIC handshake | QUIC TLS 1.3 (E2E) | QUIC |
   641→| ERC-8004 | On-chain identity verification | N/A (public data) | Blockchain consensus |
   642→
   643→### Application-Level Screening
   644→
   645→Incoming Iroh connections are screened:
   646→
   647→1. Is this NodeId in my Collective? (Check ERC-8004 `getAgentsByOperator()`)
   648→2. If not Collective, is it a known cross-collective peer? (Check ERC-8004 for valid
   649→   registration)
   650→3. If unknown, reject the connection
   651→
   652→### Trust Gradients
   653→
   654→| Source | Trust Factor | Confidence Multiplier |
   655→|--------|-------------|----------------------|
   656→| Self (own agent) | 1.0 | None |
   657→| Collective (sibling) | 0.9 | ×0.80 |
   658→| Public (anonymous) | 0.7 | ×0.50 |
   659→| Cross-collective (stranger) | 0.6 | ×0.60 |
   660→
   661→### Gossip Security
   662→
   663→Iroh-gossip messages are signed by the sender's ed25519 key. Recipients verify the signature
   664→before processing. Application-level validation (confidence bounds, domain checks, pheromone
   665→format) is applied after signature verification.
   666→
   667→### Address Privacy
   668→
   669→By default, Iroh publishes only the relay URL via pkarr (not direct IP addresses), preventing
   670→IP leakage. Configurable via `AddrFilter::relay_only()` (default) or
   671→`AddrFilter::unfiltered()`.
   672→
   673→---
   674→
   675→## Version Vectors and Deduplication
   676→
   677→Each agent maintains a version vector tracking the highest sequence number received from each
   678→source:
   679→
   680→```rust
   681→/// Version vector for deduplication and delta computation.
   682→/// Maps agent_id -> highest_seen_sequence_number.
   683→///
   684→/// Sequence numbers are u64, assigned by each agent's local counter.
   685→/// The counter is persisted to disk so it survives restarts.
   686→pub struct VersionVector {
   687→    /// Map from agent_id to highest seen sequence number.
   688→    entries: HashMap<AgentId, u64>,
   689→}
   690→
   691→/// Per-agent sequence number generator.
   692→///
   693→/// Each agent maintains a monotonically increasing counter. The counter
   694→/// is persisted to `{data_dir}/mesh_seq.u64` (8 bytes, little-endian).
   695→/// On startup, the counter loads from disk. If the file is missing or
   696→/// corrupt, the counter resets to 0 — receivers handle this via the
   697→/// wraparound recovery protocol.
   698→pub struct SeqGenerator {
   699→    current: AtomicU64,
   700→    persist_path: PathBuf,
   701→}
   702→
   703→impl SeqGenerator {
   704→    /// Allocate the next sequence number.
   705→    /// The counter increments atomically — safe for concurrent deposits.
   706→    pub fn next(&self) -> u64 {
   707→        let seq = self.current.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
   708→        // Async persist — best effort. If the write fails, the counter
   709→        // is still correct in memory. It will be re-persisted on the
   710→        // next successful write.
   711→        self.persist_async(seq + 1);
   712→        seq
   713→    }
   714→}
   715→```
   716→
   717→### Sequence number assignment
   718→
   719→Each outbound message gets a sequence number from the agent's local `SeqGenerator`. The
   720→number is embedded in the `MeshEnvelope.seq` field. Sequence numbers are per-agent, not
   721→per-topic or per-message-type: all messages from agent A share a single counter. This keeps
   722→the version vector compact (one entry per agent, not per topic).
   723→
   724→### How deduplication works
   725→
   726→1. Agent A deposits pheromone with seq=42
   727→2. Pheromone arrives via Iroh (fast): Agent B checks version vector, seq 42 is new -> process,
   728→   update vector to {A: 42}
   729→3. Same pheromone arrives via WebSocket (slower): Agent B checks version vector, seq 42
   730→   already seen -> drop silently
   731→
   732→This ensures exactly-once processing regardless of how many transports deliver the message.
   733→
   734→### Wraparound recovery
   735→
   736→A `u64` counter at 1 message per millisecond takes ~584 million years to wrap. In practice,
   737→wraparound does not happen. The recovery protocol exists for a different scenario: **counter
   738→reset** after data loss (disk failure, corrupt persist file, fresh deployment with new state).
   739→
   740→When agent A's counter resets to 0 but agent B's version vector says `{A: 5000}`, agent B
   741→would reject all of A's messages as "already seen."
   742→
   743→```
   744→Recovery protocol:
   745→1. Agent A detects its counter is lower than peers expect (peers send NACK
   746→   with their last-seen seq for A).
   747→2. Agent A broadcasts a `SeqReset` message (signed, includes new starting seq = 0
   748→   and a reset_epoch that increments on each reset).
   749→3. Receivers update their version vector: set A's entry to 0, record the new
   750→   reset_epoch. Future messages from A are accepted starting from seq 0.
   751→4. The reset_epoch prevents replay attacks: a message with an old reset_epoch
   752→   is rejected even if the seq is valid for the current epoch.
   753→```
   754→
   755→```rust
   756→/// Sent when an agent's sequence counter resets (data loss, fresh deploy).
   757→/// Receivers must update their version vector to accept messages from seq 0.
   758→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   759→pub struct SeqReset {
   760→    pub agent_id: AgentId,
   761→    /// Monotonically increasing epoch. Each reset increments this.
   762→    /// Persisted alongside the sequence counter.
   763→    pub reset_epoch: u64,
   764→    /// Ed25519 signature over (agent_id || reset_epoch).
   765→    pub signature: [u8; 64],
   766→}
   767→```
   768→
   769→### Delta sync on reconnection
   770→
   771→When an agent reconnects after being offline:
   772→
   773→1. Agent sends its current version vector to the Mesh server or peer
   774→2. Server/peer computes the delta: all Engrams with seq > the vector's value for each source
   775→3. Delta is sent to the reconnecting agent
   776→4. Agent processes the delta and updates its version vector
   777→
   778→This is efficient: only new data is transferred, and the version vector is compact (one entry
   779→per agent in the Collective).
   780→

   783→## Configuration
   784→
   785→```toml
   786→[mesh]
   787→# Master switch — false means local-only operation.
   788→enabled = true
   789→
   790→[mesh.websocket]
   791→enabled = true
   792→url = "wss://mesh.roko.dev/v1/mesh/ws"
   793→reconnect_interval_secs = 5
   794→store_forward_ttl_days = 7
   795→
   796→[mesh.iroh]
   797→enabled = true
   798→secret_key_path = "data/iroh.key"
   799→relay_mode = "default"  # "default" (n0 public), "custom", or "disabled"
   800→# relay_url = "https://relay.my-mesh.example.com"  # for relay_mode = "custom"
   801→
   802→[mesh.discovery]
   803→# ERC-8004 agent discovery
   804→erc8004_poll_interval_secs = 300
   805→cross_collective_enabled = false
   806→max_cross_collective_peers = 50
   807→
   808→[mesh.sync]
   809→# Sync timing
   810→batch_interval_ticks = 50  # Curator-aligned
   811→immediate_threshold = 0.7  # Push immediately above this intensity
   812→
   813→[mesh.budget]
   814→max_per_tick = 0.01        # Max USDC per tick
   815→daily_budget = 0.50        # Max USDC per day
   816→monthly_budget = 10.00     # Max USDC per month
   817→```
